round1_2_raw.title: write_patched_pfile does not validate header_size >= 128, producing silent header corruption or a cryptic struct.error on a corrupt/hostile source
round1_2_raw.evidence: header_size is taken verbatim from header word 17 (line 504) and used unchecked to (a) slice the copied header region `bytearray(f.read(header_size))` (line 527) and (b) patch config_size via `struct.pack_into(..., _H['config_size']*2=22, ...)` (line 528). Unlike extract_pfile_segment in p_file.py:138 (`if header_size < HEADER_BYTES: raise ValueError`), the patch writer has NO `header_size >= 128` guard. Two confirmed failure modes on a corrupt source whose header_version>=6 and file_size>first_record_size: (1) header_size in [0,23] -> raw `struct.error: pack_into requires a buffer of at least 24 bytes ... (actual buffer size is 20)`. struct.error is neither OSError nor ValueError (mro confirmed: Exception->BaseException), so the CLI handler at cli.py:291 `except (FileNotFoundError, FileExistsError, ValueError, OSError)` does NOT catch it -- the user gets a full traceback and the wrong exit behavior instead of `Error: ...`/exit 1. (2) header_size in [24,127] -> the call SUCCEEDS and writes an output .p with a truncated (<128-byte) header region; reproduced with header_size=64: the written file has only 64 header bytes before the config string, silently producing a malformed/unreadable .p. record_size (word 18) is likewise unvalidated here though it is unused by the copy.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: After unpacking the header in write_patched_pfile (and analogously in read_config_text), add the same bounds checks extract_pfile_segment already uses: `if header_size < HEADER_BYTES: raise ValueError(f'{src.name}: invalid header_size={header_size}')` and `if config_size < 0: raise ValueError(...)`. This converts both the cryptic struct.error and the silent-malformed-output cases into a clean, CLI-catchable ValueError, matching the existing reader's contract.

round1_2_raw.title: edit_config_text mis-detects a pre-existing CONFIG_MARKER inside the ORIGINAL config, silently freezing everything below it as an 'original block' and refusing edits with a misleading 'section is not present' error
round1_2_raw.evidence: edit_config_text splits the active region at the FIRST occurrence of CONFIG_MARKER (`marker_idx = config_str.find(CONFIG_MARKER)`, line 324) and treats `config_str[marker_idx:]` as a frozen, re-attached-verbatim original block that is never parsed or edited. The marker text `'; ### Original Configuration String Below ###'` is, per the module docstring, deliberately identical to RSI patch_setupstr.m's marker ('Matches RSI patch_setupstr.m so RSI -revert can still recover the original'). Therefore a .p ALREADY patched by RSI's MATLAB tool -- or any original config that merely contains this string in a descriptive comment -- will have all [channel]/[instrument_info]/[cruise_info] stanzas located BELOW the marker rendered invisible. Reproduced: a config with the marker line followed by `[cruise_info]\nship = Thompson`, edited with cruise_info.ship -> 'Sally Ride', raises `ValueError: [cruise_info] section is not present in this file` even though the section is plainly present; the edit is silently impossible. No test covers a marker that is part of the original (only the self-produced idempotent-repatch case is tested).
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Only treat text after the marker as a frozen original block when this tool produced it -- i.e. require the marker to be immediately preceded by this tool's banner bar (or a sentinel it writes), or scan that the marker line begins at column 0 and is followed by the expected `; ` commented-original pattern. At minimum, when the post-marker region does NOT look like a fully `; `-commented block, fall back to treating the whole string as active config. Alternatively, detect the RSI-vs-self origin and parse/edit the active region accordingly so RSI-pre-patched files remain editable.

round1_2_raw.title: Production epsilon viscosity ignores salinity and pressure (visc35/S=35 hardcoded), inconsistent with chi/N2 viscosity in the same pipeline run
round1_2_raw.evidence: process_l4 computes per-window viscosity as `nu = float(visc35(temp[j]))` (l4.py:176) — temperature-only, salinity hardwired to 35 PSU, pressure-independent. L3Data carries `pres` (per-window mean) and the L1 path can carry measured `salinity` (io.py:18,49), but process_l4 has no salinity/pressure parameter and discards both. The production pipeline (pipeline.py:347) calls `process_l4(l3, temp=l3.temp, ...)` for EPSILON while resolving measured/user salinity (`chi_salinity, measured_sal = _resolve_salinity(l1, salinity)`, pipeline.py:361) and feeding it to chi viscosity AND N2 stratification. So a single run uses measured S (e.g. JAC conductivity) for chi+N2 but S=35 for epsilon. The deprecated dissipation.py path DOES support salinity-aware epsilon viscosity (`visc(l3.temp, sal_window, l3.pres)`, dissipation.py:218), so the production path is a regression in capability relative to the deprecated one. Quantified bias on nu (hence ~linearly on epsilon): ~0.05% at S=30,P=0 (Arctic surface), ~0.76% at S=37,P=500dbar (deep/Mediterranean) — verified via ocean.visc vs ocean.visc35 at T=10C. Note visc35 is the documented ATOMIX benchmark reference, so the S=35 choice is benchmark-pinned for validation; the defect is the SILENT inconsistency in field data, not the benchmark value.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Plumb salinity (and pres) into process_l4: add an optional `salinity` parameter (per-window, interpolated like temp), and compute `nu = visc(temp[j], sal[j], l3.pres[j])` when salinity is provided, falling back to visc35 otherwise (exactly as dissipation.py:213-220 already does). Have pipeline.py:347 pass the resolved `chi_salinity` so epsilon and chi/N2 share one viscosity basis. Keep visc35 as the default to preserve ATOMIX benchmark parity. At minimum, document in process_l4 that epsilon viscosity is S=35-only and diverges from the chi/N2 salinity in the same run.

round1_2_raw.title: Variance-method polyfit window forced to >=7 cpm where ODAS allows down to K_95 (lower bound 0), introducing noise-floor bins into the spectral-minimum fit for very-low-epsilon windows
round1_2_raw.evidence: In _variance_method the polyfit-window upper wavenumber is `valid_K_limit = min(max(min(K_AA,K_95), K_LIMIT_MIN=7), K_LIMIT_MAX=150)` (l4.py:475-479), then `index_limit = searchsorted(K, valid_K_limit)`. ODAS get_diss_odas.m:563 computes the SAME window as `valid_shear = find(K <= fit_in_range(min([K_AA,K_95]), [0 150]))` — fit_in_range's lower bound is 0, NOT 7 (the 7-cpm floor in ODAS, get_diss_odas.m:605, applies only to the FINAL integration limit K_limit, not to the polyfit window). For epsilon <~ 2e-11 W/kg, K_95 < 7 cpm (verified: e=1e-11 -> K_95=5.4, e=1e-12 -> K_95=3.0 at nu=1.35e-6), so the Python code extends the degree-3..8 polyfit window from ~3-5 cpm up to 7 cpm, dragging noise-floor bins into the polynomial fit. That can produce spurious interior minima (np.roots, l4.py:499-507) that change the detected K_limit_log vs the K_95 fallback. The FINAL integration range is clamped to >=7 cpm in both implementations (l4.py:511 vs ODAS:605), so the integrated variance range is identical — only the spectral-minimum DETECTION differs, bounding the impact to the lowest-epsilon windows.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Match ODAS's polyfit-window bound: clip `min(K_AA, K_95)` to [0, K_LIMIT_MAX] (lower bound 0, not K_LIMIT_MIN) when computing `index_limit`, keeping the K_LIMIT_MIN=7 floor only on the final `K_limit_log` (line 511, already correct). Add a low-epsilon regression test (K_95 < 7) asserting the polyfit window matches ODAS's `find(K <= K_95)` extent.

round1_2_raw.title: process_l4 EPSI_FINAL fallback geometric-mean includes probes flagged for inter-probe inconsistency (bit 4) and FOM (bit 1), reporting a 'final' epsilon for windows where all probes failed QC
round1_2_raw.evidence: _compute_epsi_final first tries probes with `flags==0` (l4.py:771); if none pass it falls back to the geometric mean of ALL finite positive probes regardless of flag (l4.py:776-778), including probes flagged for FOM>limit (bit1) or inter-probe inconsistency (bit4, where the two probes disagree by > 1.96*sqrt(2)*sigma_ln). For a 2-probe window whose probes disagree by, say, an order of magnitude (both flagged bit4), EPSI_FINAL becomes exp(mean(log)) of two mutually-inconsistent estimates — a number with no QC backing, indistinguishable downstream from a clean estimate (no flag is propagated onto epsi_final itself). The comment frames this as intentional, but the inconsistency flag specifically marks the larger estimate as untrustworthy, so averaging it in defeats that flag.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: When falling back, prefer the window minimum (e_min, which is never the bit-4-flagged suspect) over the geometric mean of all probes, or emit a companion epsi_final_flag/quality field so downstream binning can distinguish a clean EPSI_FINAL from an all-flagged fallback. At minimum, exclude bit-4 (and optionally bit-1) probes from the fallback mean.

round1_2_raw.title: Deprecated _compute_epsilon lacks the NaN-temperature viscosity guard that process_l4 has, silently producing NaN epsilon
round1_2_raw.evidence: process_l4 (scor160/l4.py:154-160) explicitly guards window temperature: if not np.all(np.isfinite(temp)) it warns and substitutes 10 degC before computing viscosity. The deprecated RSI path _compute_epsilon does NOT: it calls nu_out = visc(l3.temp, sal_window, l3.pres) or visc35(l3.temp) directly. l3.temp can legitimately contain NaN (scor160/l3.py:181 appends np.nan when temp data is absent or l1.temp shape mismatches). I verified visc35(nan)->nan and visc(nan,...)->nan, and nasmyth_grid(epsilon, nu=nan, k) returns all-NaN WITHOUT raising (the `if nu <= 0` check at nasmyth.py:192 is False for NaN). Net effect: any window with NaN temperature yields nu=NaN -> epsilon=NaN and an all-NaN Nasmyth spectrum, with NO warning to the user, whereas the maintained pipeline path recovers gracefully with a warning. This is a behavioral inconsistency between the two epsilon entry points.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Mirror the process_l4 guard in _compute_epsilon: after computing l3.temp-derived viscosity inputs, replace non-finite temperatures with the 10 degC default and emit a warnings.warn(...), e.g. `temp = np.where(np.isfinite(l3.temp), l3.temp, 10.0)` before calling visc/visc35, matching scor160/l4.py:154-160.

round1_2_raw.title: num_ffts can go negative when diss_length < fft_length, silently disabling FM/dof_spec QC with no validation
round1_2_raw.evidence: num_ffts = 2 * (diss_length // fft_length) - 1. There is no validation that diss_length >= fft_length (L3Params in scor160/io.py has no such check, and get_diss/compute_diss_file forward user-supplied values). For a user misconfiguration diss_length=512, fft_length=1024 I confirmed num_ffts = -1. Consequences: (a) dof_spec = DOF_NUTTALL * max(num_ffts - n_v, 1) silently collapses to 1.9 (a meaningless DOF), stored as a dataset attribute; (b) num_ffts=-1 is passed to _estimate_epsilon where `if num_ffts > 0` is False, so the Lueck FM statistic is silently skipped -- and in the production pipeline.py:352 the same expression feeds process_l4, where num_ffts<=0 disables FM-based QC flagging entirely. No error or warning is raised; the spectra still compute, so the user gets epsilon estimates with QC silently degraded.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Validate at the point num_ffts is computed (and/or in L3Params construction): raise ValueError if diss_length < fft_length, or at minimum guard with num_ffts = max(2 * (diss_length // fft_length) - 1, 1) and emit a warning when diss_length < fft_length so the degraded-QC condition is visible.

round1_2_raw.title: fom variance-ratio QC flag (bit 1) does not catch negative observed variance after Goodman cleaning
round1_2_raw.evidence: When the FM statistic is unavailable (num_ffts == 0), _compute_flags uses the variance-ratio fom as fom_for_flags and flags only `fom > fom_limit` (l4.py:731). fom_val = obs_var / nas_var (l4.py:413) where obs_var = np.trapezoid(spec_safe_pos, K_pos). After Goodman coherent-noise removal, sh_spec_clean bins can be negative, so obs_var (and hence fom_val) can be negative for a genuinely bad fit. A negative fom is < fom_limit, so the window is NOT flagged by bit 1 -- a clearly bad fit passes QC. This only affects the num_ffts==0 fallback (production pipeline.py always passes num_ffts>0 and thus flags on FM instead), which bounds the impact, but the variance-ratio fallback path is reachable via process_l4(..., num_ffts=0).
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: In _compute_flags, also flag non-physical fom: e.g. `flags[(fom > fom_limit) | (fom <= 0)] += 1` (when fom_for_flags is the variance-ratio fom), or clamp obs_var/fom_val to be flagged when obs_var <= 0 in _estimate_epsilon.

round1_2_raw.title: rsi pipeline binned/combined products (L5/L6) have no units, long_name, or standard_name on any data variable
round1_2_raw.evidence: bin_by_depth() attaches only cell_methods to each data variable (binning.py:103-122) — epsilon, chi, K_T, Gamma, K_rho, P_mean, speed get NO units/long_name/standard_name. combine_profiles() then builds data_vars as (['profile','depth_bin'], combined) with no attrs dict at all (combine.py:82), so it also drops the cell_methods. Verified at runtime: bin_by_depth['epsilon'].attrs == {'cell_methods':...}; combine_profiles['epsilon'].attrs == {}. These are the headline products of run_pipeline (CLAUDE.md-documented; rsi-tpw pipeline): L5_binned.nc (pipeline.py:599) and L6_combined.nc (pipeline.py:279). Every other writer in the package (convert.py, profile.py, perturb via netcdf_schema) sets per-variable units. The L4_epsilon.nc/L4_chi.nc per-level files DO carry units, but the depth-binned/combined products that users actually analyze do not. Existing tests assert the depth_bin coordinate has units (test_binning_combine.py:201,307) but no test checks the epsilon/chi data-var units, so the gap is uncaught.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Attach CF attrs to the binned data variables. In bin_by_depth(), merge a units/long_name/standard_name dict (e.g. epsilon->W kg-1 / specific_turbulent_kinetic_energy_dissipation_in_sea_water, chi->K2 s-1, K_T/K_rho->m2 s-1, Gamma->1, speed->m s-1) into each `attrs` alongside cell_methods, keyed by variable name (reuse COMBO_SCHEMA/CHI_SCHEMA). In combine_profiles(), carry each source variable's attrs through into the (['profile','depth_bin'], combined, attrs) tuple. Add a test asserting epsilon/chi carry units after combine.

round1_2_raw.title: L6_combined.nc and L5_binned.nc lack the Conventions/CF global attribute set by every other writer
round1_2_raw.evidence: combine_profiles() returns a Dataset whose .attrs is {} (verified at runtime), and pipeline.py only adds profile_number/source_file/bin_size before to_netcdf. bin_by_depth output carries only {'bin_size':...}. By contrast convert.py:478, profile.py:148, ctd.py:415, combo.py:144, and both _write_l4_* set Conventions='CF-1.13, ACDD-1.3'. The depth-binned headline products therefore advertise no CF conformance, so a CF/ACDD consumer cannot validate them and won't apply the convention's coordinate semantics.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Set combined.attrs['Conventions']='CF-1.13, ACDD-1.3' (and ideally featureType='profile') in combine_profiles or in pipeline.py before combined.to_netcdf; likewise add Conventions to the L5 binned dataset attrs in _process_profile.

round1_2_raw.title: L1 supplementary channels written with raw RSI units (e.g. 'deg', 'deg_C', 'mS_cm-1') bypassing canonicalize_units
round1_2_raw.evidence: The supplementary-channel loop writes v.units = info['units'] verbatim (convert.py:541). RSI's channel converters emit non-UDUNITS strings: convert_inclxy returns 'deg' (channels.py:220), convert_jac_c 'mS_cm-1' (:242), convert_aroft_o2 'umol_L-1' (:265), inclinometer/thermistor 'deg_C'. The per-profile writer fixes exactly this by calling canonicalize_units on the units attr (profile.py:244-246) using netcdf_schema.UNITS_FIXUP, but the L1 ATOMIX writer (the documented `rsi-tpw nc` primary product) does not. Verified UDUNITS rejects 'deg', 'mS_cm-1', 'umol_L-1' (cf_units raises UT_UNKNOWN); for SN479 the supplementary set is Gnd/Incl_T/PV/V_Bat (Incl_T='deg_C' is borderline), but configs with Incl_X/Incl_Y ('deg') or extra conductivity write hard-invalid units. test_cf_compliance (test_convert.py:149) does not run a UDUNITS check so it is uncaught.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Import canonicalize_units from odas_tpw.perturb.netcdf_schema and apply it at convert.py:541, i.e. v.units = canonicalize_units(info['units']), matching the per-profile path.

round1_2_raw.title: L1 MAG variable declares units='micro_Tesla', not UDUNITS-parseable
round1_2_raw.evidence: The MAG spec hardcodes units='micro_Tesla' (convert.py:361). cf_units.Unit('micro_Tesla') raises UT_UNKNOWN (verified) — UDUNITS has no 'micro_Tesla' alias; the parseable forms are 'uT' or 'microtesla'. This is in a core hardcoded L1 variable (not a passthrough), so it is wrong for any magnetometer-equipped instrument regardless of the canonicalize_units gap above. (No magnetometer in the SN479 config, so latent for this dataset.)
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Change units to 'uT' (preferred) or 'microtesla'. Add the magnetic-field standard_name as a comment only — 'magnetic_field' is also not a CF standard name.

round1_2_raw.title: CTD per-file NetCDF writes _FillValue on the 'time' coordinate variable (CF-1.13 §2.5.1 violation)
round1_2_raw.line: 375-419 (ds.to_netcdf with no _FillValue:None encoding on time coord)
round1_2_raw.evidence: ctd_bin_file builds time as an xarray coordinate and calls ds.to_netcdf(out_path) with no encoding (ctd.py:419). xarray auto-emits a _FillValue on float coordinates; verified the written 'time' var carries _FillValue in its ncattrs. CF-1.13 §2.5.1 forbids _FillValue on coordinate variables. combo.py:286-291 explicitly strips this via encoding={cname:{'_FillValue':None}} for exactly this reason, but the per-file CTD writer does not. Lower severity because make_combo later re-reads with decode_times=False and re-writes the combo (stripping coords), so the final CTD combo is clean — but the intermediate ctd/*.nc products are non-compliant.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Pass encoding={'time': {'_FillValue': None}} (and ideally None for any other coordinate) to ds.to_netcdf in ctd_bin_file, mirroring make_combo.

round1_2_raw.title: PR #78 _nc_filled fix turns a truncated cast into a SILENTLY LOST cast: one mid-cast _FillValue NaNs the entire fall-rate via gradient+filtfilt, so zero profiles are detected for the whole file
round1_2_raw.evidence: The M2 fix replaces masked _FillValue (~9.97e36) with NaN at all read sites. Its stated rationale was that the raw fill 'truncated the rest of the cast'. But a single NaN in slow pressure is now smeared across the ENTIRE array by smooth_fall_rate(): W = np.gradient(P) puts NaN in the two neighbors, then the zero-phase Butterworth filtfilt spreads NaN to every sample. I reproduced end-to-end: a 2000-sample synthetic down-cast with exactly ONE mid-cast P fill yields `No profiles found in cast` / `profiles found with 1 mid-cast fill: 0`, whereas the identical file with the fill written yields a normal profile. Standalone proof: P=linspace with P[100]=NaN -> filtfilt(W) has `finite count after filtfilt: 0 of 200`. So the fix converts 'rest of cast truncated' into 'whole cast silently lost' — arguably a worse failure. The M2 regression test (tests/test_audit_2026_06_25_fixes.py::TestM2FillValueLeak) only asserts P[5] becomes NaN; it never calls extract_profiles, so this downstream regression is uncovered.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: After _nc_filled, before profile detection, repair NaNs in the slow pressure (and any time coordinate) used for fall-rate: e.g. linearly interpolate isolated NaNs in P over its index (np.interp on the finite subset) inside _load_from_nc, or have smooth_fall_rate operate on a NaN-interpolated copy. At minimum, count NaNs and warn/log when >0 so a poisoned cast is visible rather than silently dropped. Add an extract_profiles end-to-end regression with a mid-cast fill that asserts the profile is still found.

round1_2_raw.title: extract_profiles crashes with AttributeError on external/partial CF NetCDF whose channel carries a _FillValue attribute — exactly the files PR #78's _nc_filled fix claims to support
round1_2_raw.line: 243-246 (blind setattr of every attr), populated from var.ncattrs() at 440-442
round1_2_raw.category: edge-case crash / silent-loss-becomes-crash on supported input
round1_2_raw.evidence: _scan_vars (line 440-442) copies ALL of var.ncattrs() into each channel's attrs dict, including '_FillValue'. The per-profile write loop creates the new variable WITHOUT fill_value (line 240) then does `setattr(var, k, v)` for every attr (line 246). netCDF4 forbids setting _FillValue after creation, so it raises `AttributeError: _FillValue attribute must be set when variable is created`. Reproduced: a fully-written full-record .nc with a channel `T1` created using `fill_value=-999.0` crashes extract_profiles with that exact AttributeError. This is precisely the 'external / partial (CF/ATOMIX) files that declare a _FillValue' class the _nc_filled docstring says it supports — the read side now tolerates fills but extract_profiles still aborts on those very files. The package's own L1/PFile path is unaffected (its attrs never include _FillValue), which is why tests miss it.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Skip reserved/unsettable attributes when copying: in the write loop, `for k, v in attrs.items(): if k in {"_FillValue", "_Netcdf4Coordinates", "_Netcdf4Dimid", "_Unsigned"}: continue`. Better: pass the source _FillValue through to createVariable(..., fill_value=...) so masked data round-trips. Add a test that extract_profiles handles a .nc whose channels declare _FillValue.

round1_2_raw.title: _nc_filled makes the days->seconds time-axis NaN-fragile: a _FillValue in the TIME coordinate poisons the whole time vector via (t - t[0]) and breaks every np.interp against it
round1_2_raw.evidence: After _nc_filled, t_fast/t_slow can contain NaN for any TIME element carrying a _FillValue. Line 453 `t_fast = (t_fast - t_fast[0]) * 86400.0`: if the first element is a fill, the entire vector becomes NaN (verified: `(t - t[0])` with t[0]=NaN -> all NaN). Even an interior NaN in the time axis breaks downstream np.interp (e.g. convert.py P_fast = np.interp(t_fast, t_slow, ...) and binning): `np.interp([...], t_with_nan, y)` returns all-NaN. The L1 group path uses 'Days since ...' units so it goes through this branch. Package-written L1 has no _FillValue on TIME (no-op), but the fix explicitly targets external/partial CF files where TIME may declare one.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Use a NaN-robust epoch reference: `t0 = np.nanmin(t_fast)` (or the first finite element) instead of t_fast[0], and interpolate/repair NaNs in the time coordinate before using it as an interpolation abscissa, or raise a clear error if the time axis is not finite-and-monotonic.

round1_2_raw.title: PR #78 M1 _variance_correction grid is ~22x slower per call (always 80,000 points) — a performance regression in the per-window, per-iteration chi loop
round1_2_raw.evidence: The fix sets n = max(int(n_fine), _K_SPAN_KB*_PTS_PER_KB) = max(2000, 80000) = 80000 unconditionally (n_fine is always dominated, hence also dead — see already-confirmed nit). _variance_correction is called once per iteration of the up-to-3-pass refinement loop (chi.py:588, also 261/440) for every chi window of every probe of every profile. Timed: new 80000-point grid = 0.91 ms/call vs old 2000-point = 0.04 ms/call (~22x). The numerical motivation is sound (the steep peak near kB must be resolved), but resolution should scale with kB, not be a flat 80000: the goal is dK ~ kB/_PTS_PER_KB over [0, 40*kB], which is achieved by the dK spacing regardless of total point count — the 80000 floor is pure overhead.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Drop the n_fine floor and size the grid by span/resolution directly: `n = int(round(_K_SPAN_KB * _PTS_PER_KB))` is what is used, but reduce _PTS_PER_KB (e.g. 200/kB already gives <0.05% per my V_total check) or cache the kB-grid across the 3 iterations when kB changes little. Verified V_total error of the 40*kB span is ~3e-4% and variance beyond 40*kB is ~0 for both Batchelor and Kraichnan, so a coarser per-kB grid keeps accuracy while cutting cost.

round1_2_raw.title: convert_all flattens input paths to basename under output_dir, so same-named .p files in different directories silently collide on one .nc — corrupting output under parallel jobs, overwriting under serial
round1_2_raw.evidence: When output_dir is set, nc_path = output_dir / pf_path.with_suffix('.nc').name uses only the basename. Two inputs VMP/a/SN479_0001.p and VMP/b/SN479_0001.p both map to output_dir/SN479_0001.nc (verified). With jobs>1 the ProcessPoolExecutor workers race writing the SAME file -> partial/corrupt NetCDF; with jobs=1 the second silently overwrites the first with no warning. The API accepts arbitrary path lists (cli passes globs), so this is reachable even though the current ARCTERX layout (one dir) doesn't trigger it.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Detect output-name collisions before dispatch: build the work list, and if two distinct sources map to the same nc_path, raise a clear ValueError (or disambiguate by including a path-derived prefix). At minimum log a warning and skip the duplicate.

round1_2_raw.title: parse_config raises a bare ValueError on a corrupt matrix row or channel id token, with no file context
round1_2_raw.evidence: Line 249 `result['matrix'].append([int(x) for x in val.split()])` and line 390 `ids = [int(x) for x in ch['id'].replace(',', ' ').split()]` apply int() directly to tokens from the embedded ASCII config string (untrusted/possibly-corrupt). A non-numeric token raises `ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'x'` (reproduced) with no filename or section context, crashing the whole PFile read. This is the config-string analogue of the already-flagged binary-header diagnostics gaps but on a separate parse path.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Wrap these int() conversions with a try/except that re-raises a ValueError naming the file, section (matrix/channel), and offending token, or skip/warn on malformed rows the way unknown converter types are handled.

round1_2_raw.title: PFile uses np.fromfile(count=n_records) where n_records is derived from a prior file-size stat — a concurrent truncation yields a silent short read and then a cryptic reshape error
round1_2_raw.evidence: file_size is taken at line 348, n_records computed at 349, then at 376 `records = np.fromfile(f, dtype=record_dtype, count=n_records)`. np.fromfile silently returns fewer elements than requested if the file is shorter (verified: asked 100, got 10). If the file shrinks between the stat and the read (concurrent writer/truncation), records.shape[0] < n_records, so total_scans (from the stale n_records) is too large and `records['data'].reshape(total_scans, self.n_cols)` at 379 raises 'cannot reshape array of size N into shape (...)'. Narrow (requires concurrent truncation), hence low confidence.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: After np.fromfile, assert len(records) == n_records (or recompute total_scans from records.shape[0]) and raise a clear 'file shrank during read / truncated' error if short.

round1_2_raw.title: Edit-spec keys are never validated, allowing config-stanza injection that defeats the unaddressable-acquisition-params guarantee
round1_2_raw.evidence:
_check_keymap() runs every spec VALUE through _check_value() (which forbids newlines, ';', non-ASCII), but the KEY is only checked against RESERVED_KEY ('name') and otherwise passed through verbatim (out[key] = _check_value(value, ...)). edit_config_text then writes an added key with f"{key} = {new_value}{eol}" (line 386). A key containing a newline is split across config lines on write, injecting arbitrary stanzas. Reproduced end-to-end through the documented CLI path (rsi-tpw patch-config --edits k.yaml --add-keys): a YAML edit spec
    note: "x"
    instrument_info:
      "a = 1\n[root]\nrate": "9"
loads cleanly (the double-quoted key with \n escapes is valid YAML), is never validated, and parse_config of the resulting patched config returns root.rate == '9'. This injects an acquisition parameter ([root] rate) into a fresh stanza — exactly the corruption the module docstring (lines 17-20, 130-137) promises is impossible because '[matrix]/[root] ... cannot be addressed'. Keys containing '=' or ';' similarly corrupt or silently drop the written line.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Validate keys the same way values are validated. In _check_keymap, after stripping, reject any key containing a newline/control char, '=', ';', '[' or ']', or non-ASCII (reuse the _check_value control-char/ASCII checks plus an explicit ' =;[] ' blacklist) BEFORE storing it. This closes the injection on both the YAML path (load_edit_spec) and the public EditSpec/edit_config_text path.

round1_2_raw.title: Provenance comment generator and the 'when' field are not control-char-sanitized, unlike the banner (defense-in-depth gap / latent injection)
round1_2_raw.evidence:
_build_banner deliberately runs author/note/source_label through _one_line() (lines 455, with an explicit comment at 451-454 that 'a stray newline ... cannot spill past the ; prefix and become active config'). The provenance() inner function (lines 350-357) interpolates spec.author, spec.note, ch.old, ch.new into a '; [PATCH ...]' comment with NO such sanitization, and the 'when' parameter is interpolated raw in BOTH provenance (line 351) and the banner (line 459) — _one_line is never applied to it. Reproduced: edit_config_text(cfg, EditSpec(note='ok
[root]
rate=7', author='a', ...), when='W') yields parse_config(...)['root']['rate'] == '7' (via the unsanitized provenance line), and edit_config_text(..., when='W
[root]
rate=9') yields root.rate == '9' (via both lines). The shipped CLI is NOT exploitable today (load_edit_spec validates note/author for control chars, and _cmd_patch_config passes a machine-generated 'when'=datetime.strftime), but edit_config_text/patch_files are documented public API and EditSpec is a public dataclass whose fields are unvalidated, so a directly-constructed spec or a future caller passing a non-trivial 'when' re-opens the exact hole the banner sanitizer was added to close.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Apply _one_line() to 'when' in both _build_banner (line 459) and provenance (line 351), and to spec.author/spec.note/ch.old/ch.new inside provenance() before interpolation. Equivalently, move all comment-line field sanitization into a single helper used by both the banner and provenance so the two paths can't diverge again.

round1_2_raw.title: rsi-tpw patch-config crashes with an uncaught traceback on malformed YAML (YAMLError not caught)
round1_2_raw.evidence: _cmd_patch_config wraps load_edit_spec+patch_files in try/except (FileNotFoundError, FileExistsError, ValueError, OSError) to print 'Error: ...' and sys.exit(1). But load_edit_spec parses the YAML with ruamel, which raises ruamel.yaml.YAMLError subclasses (ParserError/ScannerError) on malformed input — none of which subclass ValueError/OSError. Reproduced: running 'rsi-tpw patch-config <fixture>.p --edits badsyntax.yaml --out ...' with edits content 'note: "x"\ninstrument_info: {bad yaml\n' escapes the handler and surfaces 'UNCAUGHT ParserError' (a full traceback to the user) instead of the intended friendly error + exit(1). Malformed YAML is a very common user error, so the dedicated error handler is defeated exactly when it is most needed.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Either catch ruamel.yaml.YAMLError in _cmd_patch_config's except tuple (import it locally), or wrap the yaml.load() call inside load_edit_spec in try/except YAMLError and re-raise as ValueError(f"{path.name}: invalid YAML: {e}") so the existing ValueError handler reports it cleanly.

round1_2_raw.title: ct_align NaN guard discards the ENTIRE profile on a single isolated bad sample, silently wiping out CT alignment for a whole file
round1_2_raw.evidence: The PR #78 (commit 1a78fca, M3) guard `if not (np.all(np.isfinite(seg_T)) and np.all(np.isfinite(seg_C))): continue` rejects the WHOLE profile segment if even one sample is non-finite. But the documented NaN source, convert_jac_c (channels.py:238 `ratio = np.where(v_part == 0, np.nan, ...)`), emits ISOLATED per-sample NaN for transient corrupt conductivity samples (v_part==0 bit errors), not whole-segment corruption. There is no NaN interpolation upstream of ct_align — pipeline.py:1049 passes the raw `pf.channels[C_name]` straight in. I reproduced the failure: a file whose 3 profiles each carry ONE isolated NaN conductivity sample now returns lag=0.0 (identity, no alignment at all) because per_profile ends up empty, whereas the true consensus lag recoverable from the finite samples is 0.0469 s (3 samples). Output: `lag with one isolated NaN per profile: 0.0 / C unchanged (identity): True / lag if isolated NaNs were interpolated: 0.046875`. The pre-fix `norm<=0` guard and the MATLAB reference would NOT have wiped the whole file. The M3 regression test (test_audit_2026_06_25_fixes.py:114) only exercises the case where a CLEAN profile co-exists and dominates, so this over-discard mode is untested. Net effect: silent loss of all C/T lag alignment -> degraded salinity/density/N2 for the entire cast whenever every profile has even one transient bad sample.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Exclude/repair only the bad samples, not the whole segment. Before computing dx/dy, drop or linearly interpolate the non-finite samples of seg_T/seg_C (e.g. mask = np.isfinite(seg_T) & np.isfinite(seg_C); require mask.sum() >= 10 and a sufficiently low NaN fraction, then interpolate the few bad points or operate on the finite-masked diff). Only `continue` when the finite fraction is too low to carry alignment information. Add a regression test where every profile has an isolated NaN and assert the consensus lag is still recovered (not 0.0).

round1_2_raw.title: detect_bottom_crash all-NaN-depth RuntimeWarning escapes the surrounding np.errstate, producing spurious log noise on legitimate all-NaN segments
round1_2_raw.evidence: Line 89-90 wraps `max_depth = np.nanmax(depth)` in `with np.errstate(invalid="ignore"):` evidently intending to silence the all-NaN-slice warning. But np.errstate only governs IEEE FP flags (invalid/divide/over/under), NOT numpy's Python-level `_methods` RuntimeWarning. I confirmed: `with np.errstate(invalid='ignore'): np.nanmax(np.full(5, np.nan))` still raises `RuntimeWarning: All-NaN slice encountered`. The existing test test_processing_bottom.py::test_all_nan_depth_returns_none passes but emits this warning (visible in the pytest run). The result is correctly handled (line 93 bails on non-finite max_depth), so this is only log noise — but it reveals a misconception about errstate scope that could mask a genuinely meaningful warning if copied elsewhere.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Suppress the actual warning category, e.g. guard with `if depth.size == 0 or not np.any(np.isfinite(depth)): return None` before calling nanmax, or wrap in `with warnings.catch_warnings(): warnings.simplefilter('ignore', RuntimeWarning)`. Update the comment to stop implying errstate suppresses it.

round1_2_raw.title: despike crashes with cryptic scipy ValueError on length-2 input (padded array exactly 6 samples; MATLAB filtfilt succeeds)
round1_2_raw.evidence: For a length-2 input, pad_len = min(2, 2*int(fs/smooth)) = 2, so dv_padded has 2*2+2 = 6 samples. scipy.signal.filtfilt with the default padlen for a 1st-order Butterworth (len(a)=len(b)=2) is 3*max(len(a),len(b)) = 6, and filtfilt requires len(x) > padlen (strictly). 6 is not > 6, so line 110 raises 'ValueError: The length of the input vector x must be greater than padlen, which is 6'. length-1 also fails (padded=3). Verified at runtime: despike(np.zeros(2), 512.0) raises ValueError. The MATLAB reference (odas/despike.m:183-184) uses MATLAB filtfilt whose default padlen for the same 1st-order filter is 3*(max(len(a),len(b))-1) = 3, so MATLAB succeeds on the length-2 (padded=6) case. This is a hard crash, not merely the numerical padlen divergence already noted in round 1. despike is a public API and is called per-section on temperature in chi/l2_chi.py:105 with temp_fast[ci, mask]; a degenerate short section would crash chi L2.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Pass an explicit padlen matching the MATLAB convention, e.g. filtfilt(b, a, dv_padded, padlen=3*(max(len(a_hp),len(b_hp))-1)) (=3 for the 1st-order filters), which simultaneously restores port-faithfulness AND avoids the crash for length-2 inputs. Alternatively guard with `if len(dv_padded) <= scipy_padlen: return dv, empty` and emit a warning.

round1_2_raw.title: A single NaN sample silently disables ALL spike detection for the whole array (real spikes pass through into chi/epsilon)
round1_2_raw.evidence: filtfilt propagates a NaN globally: if any element of dv_padded is NaN, dv_hp (line 110) and dv_lp (line 114) become all-NaN, so ratio = dv_hp/dv_lp (line 118) is all-NaN and `ratio > thresh` (line 119) is all-False. No spikes are detected ANYWHERE, so despike silently no-ops and any genuine spikes survive. Verified at runtime: a 10000-sample signal with a real 50x spike at index 3000 and a single NaN at index 7000 returns spikes=[] / passes=0 / frac=0.0 and y[3000] is left at 50.0 (NOT removed). The round-1 NaN fix (lines 81-85) only corrected the *fraction* reporting (NaN!=NaN inflation); it masked this deeper integrity problem by making the no-op look clean (frac=0.0). chi/l2_chi.py:105 feeds despike raw L1 FP07 temperature per section; an FP07 dropout/saturation NaN therefore leaves un-despiked spikes that contaminate the temperature-gradient spectrum and bias chi, with no diagnostic. (MATLAB filtfilt also propagates NaN, but ODAS upstream removes/interpolates NaNs before despiking; the port does not.)
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Detect non-finite input up front: if not np.all(np.isfinite(y)), either (a) interpolate/zero-fill the gaps before filtering and restore NaNs afterward, or (b) at minimum emit a warning and skip detection only over the contaminated contiguous span rather than the whole array. A NaN-guard at the top of despike() that warns is the minimum acceptable fix so the silent no-op is observable.

round1_2_raw.title: Replacement value falls back to physical 0.0 (not NaN) when a bad region has no valid neighbors, silently injecting a plausible-looking shear/temperature value
round1_2_raw.evidence: When both the before-region (line 148-151) and after-region (line 154-155) collect zero valid points, the port sets replacement = 0.0 (line 164) and writes it into dv_padded[s_bad:e_bad] (line 166). The MATLAB reference (odas/despike.m:236-245) computes start_value = sum(points)/length(points); with empty `points` this is 0/0 = NaN, so the replacement (start_value+stop_value)/2 is NaN — a value downstream QC can detect and reject. The port instead emits 0.0, a physically plausible zero shear/temperature-gradient value that silently passes through all NaN-based rejection. Verified reachable at runtime: with a large removal window N and densely packed spikes covering the whole padded array, _single_despike output is entirely 0.0 with no NaN. This is a genuine port divergence in edge handling that converts a detectable failure (NaN) into silent fabricated data (0.0).
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Match ODAS: use replacement = np.nan when neither side has valid points (and ideally also when only one side is empty, since MATLAB's (NaN+value)/2 = NaN means MATLAB does NOT one-side-fall-back either — the one-sided branches on lines 159-162 are themselves a divergence). At minimum change line 164 to `replacement = np.nan` so the unfillable region is detectable rather than silently zeroed.

round1_2_raw.title: ct_align silently applies zero CT alignment when sporadic NaN conductivity poisons every profile (PR #78 guard regression)
round1_2_raw.evidence: PR #78 (commit 1a78fca) added the guard `if not (np.all(np.isfinite(seg_T)) and np.all(np.isfinite(seg_C))): continue` at line 83. The guard is correct in intent (one NaN is smeared across the segment by the IIR lfilter at line 86-87, making `norm` NaN). But it is all-or-nothing: a SINGLE non-finite sample drops the ENTIRE profile from the weighted median. JAC/FP07 conductivity commonly has isolated NaN spikes (the module's own comment at line 81 notes `convert_jac_c emits NaN for corrupt conductivity`). If every profile carries one such spike, `per_profile` ends empty -> line 119-120 returns `C.copy(), 0.0` (no alignment), or `total<=0` -> line 130-132 returns the same. The module imports no logger (only numpy + scipy), so the fallback to zero-lag is completely silent — the CTD product is shipped un-aligned with no diagnostic. This regresses discoverability versus the pre-guard behavior, which at least produced *some* lag estimate.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Two-part fix. (1) Before the all-finite reject, interpolate over isolated interior NaNs (e.g. np.interp on finite indices) so a single bad sample does not discard an otherwise-usable profile; only `continue` when too few finite samples remain. (2) Add module logging and emit a `logger.warning(...)` on the two silent fallback paths (lines 119-120 and 130-132) reporting how many profiles were skipped and that no CT shift was applied, so an all-skipped degradation is visible.

round1_2_raw.title: pair_nearest aligns epsilon onto chi grid by time only, discarding Gamma/K_rho when the nearest epsilon window is a NaN-rejected estimate
round1_2_raw.evidence: pair_nearest selects the temporally nearest source index (`pick`) and returns `sv[pick]` without checking whether that value is finite. Epsilon estimates (l4.epsi_final / epsilonMean) are NaN for QC-rejected shear windows (scor160/l4.py initializes epsi to np.nan and leaves rejected windows NaN). When a chi window coincides with a rejected epsilon window, pair_nearest returns NaN even when a perfectly good epsilon estimate exists at the adjacent window within max_dt. Demonstrated: src_t=[0,8,16], src_v=[1e-7, nan, 3e-7], dst=[8] -> returns [nan], although valid epsilon sits one median-spacing (8 s = max_dt) away. mixing_coefficients then masks Gamma and K_rho to NaN for that window (mixing.py:481-486, requires eps_ok). This needlessly thins Gamma/K_rho coverage in low-SNR data where epsilon rejections are common, while K_T (chi-only) survives — an asymmetric, silent loss of the very coefficients the module exists to produce.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Make pair_nearest prefer the nearest FINITE source value within max_dt. E.g. mask non-finite src_values out of the candidate set (set their effective distance to +inf before the left/right pick, or operate on the finite-only subset of (st, sv)) so a rejected epsilon window does not shadow a valid neighbor. Add a regression test mirroring the demonstrated case.

round1_2_raw.title: K_rho N2_min floor (1e-9 s^-2) only prevents division-by-zero, not unphysical inflation; near-floor windows emit absurd Osborn diffusivities
round1_2_raw.evidence: K_rho = gamma_osborn * epsilon / N2 is emitted whenever `N2 >= N2_min` with N2_min=1e-9 s^-2 (DEFAULT_N2_MIN, line 57). At N2 just above the floor the Osborn scaling diverges: eps=1e-7 W/kg, N2=2e-9 -> K_rho = 0.2*1e-7/2e-9 = 10 m^2/s, ~4-5 orders above any realistic ocean diapycnal diffusivity (1e-5 to 1e-3 m^2/s). N (~0.026 cph at 2e-9) means the water is effectively unstratified, where Osborn does not physically apply, yet the value is reported as finite and would survive the `K_rho > 0` plot filter (mixing_look.py:323). The module docstring acknowledges divergence as N->0 and the floor is 'configurable', so this is partly by-design, but the chosen floor is low enough to leak physically meaningless diffusivities into the product rather than mask them.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Either raise DEFAULT_N2_MIN to a value where the Osborn estimate is still meaningful (e.g. ~1e-7 s^-2, N~0.26 cph) or add an explicit upper sanity clamp/mask on K_rho (and K_T) at the ~1e-1 m^2/s scale, documenting the choice. At minimum, surface the count of near-floor windows so consumers know the estimate is divergent there.

round1_2_raw.title: Iterative/MLE chi fit discards a valid earlier kB when a later refinement step destabilizes to NaN, silently dropping the whole window
round1_2_raw.evidence: In `_iterative_fit`, iteration 0 can produce a finite kB>=1 (sets kB_prev, computes a corrected chi_obs), but the loop reuses the *refined* chi_obs to re-run `_mle_find_kB` on the next pass. If that second call destabilizes and returns kB=NaN or kB<1, the guard `if not np.isfinite(kB_best) or kB_best < 1: break` (l.557) breaks with `kB_best` holding the *bad* iteration-1 value, overwriting the valid iteration-0 kB. Lines 599-607 then see `np.isfinite(kB_best)==False`, so `epsilon=NaN`, `chi=NaN`, `spec_batch=zeros`, and the window is discarded entirely — even though iteration 0 had a perfectly good fit and a finite chi_obs. The l4_chi.py MLE-iteration loop (added by the M-6 fix, commit 8de08e1) has the identical flaw and is worse: line 267 *overwrites* `result` with the new `_mle_fit_kB` output before the next loop's guard can reject it, so a destabilized NaN result is returned and `kB_val=result.kB=NaN` discards the valid prior estimate. Demonstrated by monkeypatching `_mle_find_kB`/`_mle_fit_kB` to return valid-then-NaN: both paths return kB=NaN, chi=NaN despite a valid iteration-0 kB=50. The feedback chi changes the coarse-grid NLL landscape, so destabilization is realistic on borderline/noisy windows — exactly the windows whose presence/absence skews QC statistics.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Keep the last valid estimate across iterations. In `_iterative_fit`, snapshot the last finite kB before re-running the MLE (e.g. only update `kB_best` if the new fit is finite and >=1, otherwise `break` retaining `kB_prev`); compute epsilon/chi from that retained kB. In l4_chi.py, save `last_good = result` before line 267's overwrite and restore it when the refined result is non-finite/<=0, mirroring the convergence guard's intent.

round1_2_raw.title: rsi run_pipeline mixing quantities use the unfiltered chi_final geometric mean (no fom/K_max_ratio QC), unlike the epsilon path and perturb's mk_chi_mean
round1_2_raw.evidence: `_compute_chi_final` (l4_chi.py l.286-294) computes `chi_final[j] = exp(mean(log(chi[good,j])))` over all probes with finite chi>0, with NO filtering on the per-probe `fom` (obs/model variance ratio) or `K_max_ratio` (spectral resolution). pipeline.py:466 then feeds `chi_primary.chi_final` straight into `mixing_coefficients(...)` -> K_T = chi/(2 dT/dz^2), Gamma = N2*chi/(2 eps dT/dz^2). The epsilon driving the same products is QC-filtered (mk_epsilon_mean / epsi_final), and the campaign (perturb) pipeline runs `mk_chi_mean` which does CI-based probe removal — so a thermistor window with a poor spectral fit (high fom, or K_max_ratio<0.5 meaning most variance is extrapolated) biases K_T/Gamma in the rsi path only. The chi output variables already carry per-window fom and K_max_ratio, so the QC information exists but is unused at the combine step.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Before the geometric mean in `_compute_chi_final` (or at the pipeline.py mixing call site), drop per-probe chi where fom exceeds the chi fom limit or K_max_ratio < ~0.5, falling back to the all-probe mean only if no probe survives — mirroring `_epsilon_ds_to_l4data`'s fom-based exclusion and processing.mk_chi_mean.

round1_2_raw.title: _iterative_fit returns a chi computed against a one-iteration-stale kB on convergence break
round1_2_raw.evidence: When the convergence test `abs(kB_best - kB_prev)/kB_prev < 0.01` (l.561) fires, the loop breaks *before* recomputing chi_obs/correction for the newly-converged `kB_best`. The returned `kB_best` (and the epsilon derived from it at l.600) is from the final MLE pass, but the returned `chi` (= chi_obs, l.606) was last assigned using the *previous* iteration's kB (k_l_new and the variance correction both depend on kB). So the reported (kB, epsilon) and (chi) correspond to two kB values differing by <1%. The mismatch is bounded (<~1-2% on chi) but means spec_batch = grad_func(K, kB_best, chi) mixes a converged kB with a slightly-stale chi.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: After the loop, recompute chi_obs once from the final kB_best (chi_band over [k_l,k_u] with k_l derived from kB_best, times _variance_correction(kB_best,...)), so the returned kB, epsilon, chi, and spec_batch are mutually consistent.

round1_2_raw.title: _bilinear_correction raises ValueError for small config-derived diff_gain (cutoff >= 1)
round1_2_raw.evidence: `butter(1, 1/(2*np.pi*diff_gain*fs/2))` (l.644) requires the normalized cutoff to lie in (0,1). With fs=512 this needs diff_gain > 1/(pi*fs) ≈ 6.2e-4; a diff_gain at/below that (e.g. a corrupt or patched .p-file config value) makes the cutoff >= 1 and scipy raises `ValueError: Digital filter critical frequencies must be 0 < Wn < 1`, aborting L3 chi for the whole cast. Confirmed: diff_gain=1e-4, fs=512 -> cutoff 6.22 -> ValueError. diff_gain is read from the instrument config (default 0.94), so the trusted-RSI likelihood is low, but the config can now be patched via rsi-tpw patch-config.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Clamp/validate the normalized cutoff before calling butter (e.g. if `wn <= 0 or wn >= 1`, warn and return an all-ones correction), so an out-of-range diff_gain degrades to no bilinear correction rather than crashing the cast.

round1_2_raw.title: longitude/latitude x-axis passthrough is not dateline-safe: a continuous track crossing +/-180 is split and reordered into two far-apart clusters
round1_2_raw.evidence: compute() for method 'latitude'/'longitude' returns the raw degree values verbatim (x=np.asarray(lon, float)). The distance/along_line/signed_distance methods were all hardened for the dateline via _circmean_deg + _wrap_deg (tested in test_*_dateline_*), but the plain latitude/longitude passthrough was not. Verified empirically: a continuous monotone track lon=[179, 179.5, -179.5, -179] (physically ~1 deg wide) yields x=[179, 179.5, -179.5, -179]; profiles.py sorts columns by x -> [-179.5, -179, 179, 179.5] (physical cast order reversed) and layout.column_clusters() splits it into [(0,2),(2,4)] with a spurious ~358-deg blank gap. scalar.py's grid path would likewise spread the data to both ends of a 360-deg-wide x-axis with an empty middle. The result silently misregisters cast order and depth-by-x position on a dateline-crossing leg.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: When method is 'longitude' (and for the longitude component of any spatial axis used for ordering), unwrap the longitudes about their circular mean before returning, e.g. lon_unwrapped = xaxis._circmean_deg(lon) + xaxis._wrap_deg(lon - _circmean_deg(lon)). This keeps a near-dateline track contiguous and monotone while leaving non-crossing tracks unchanged. Add a regression test mirroring test_signed_distance_dateline_span_matches_haversine for the longitude method. (Low urgency for the current ARCTERX/Saipan ~145E data which never crosses the dateline.)

round1_2_raw.title: eps_chi profile realignment over-reports 'eps slots have no chi' by counting validly-matched but all-NaN chi columns as unmatched
round1_2_raw.evidence: After mapping each chi column onto its nearest eps slot, n_unmatched = int(np.sum(~np.isfinite(aligned).any(axis=0))). A chi profile that was successfully matched (within 5 s) but is entirely NaN over depth -- a legitimate outcome after QC dropping every bin, or an FP07 cast with no valid chi -- has no finite values, so this counts it as 'no chi match'. The printed diagnostic ('N eps slots have no chi') then overstates the number of genuinely unmatched-in-time profiles, which could mislead a user debugging an apparent chi shortfall.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Track matches explicitly rather than inferring them from finiteness: maintain a boolean matched = np.zeros(len(t_eps), bool), set matched[k]=True inside the 'if abs(...) <= 5' branch, and report n_unmatched = int((~matched).sum()). This counts time-unmatched eps slots correctly regardless of whether the matched chi column happens to be all-NaN.

round1_2_raw.title: L4 NaN-speed window yields wrong-but-finite epsilon: K_AA derived from un-floored speed (max(NaN,0.05)=NaN)
round1_2_raw.evidence: process_l4 reads W = l3.pspd_rel[j] (the un-floored per-window speed) and computes K_AA = 0.9 * f_AA / max(W, 0.05). In Python, max(np.nan, 0.05) returns nan (the comparison 0.05 > nan is False, so the first arg is kept) — verified at runtime. l3.py:212 deliberately floors W to 0.05 ONLY for the wavenumber axis (W = max(np.nan_to_num(W, nan=0.05), 0.05)) but stores the un-floored mean in pspd_rel (l3.py:184-189, comment '#59' explicitly notes an all-NaN window records NaN). So an all-NaN-speed window reaches L4 with W=NaN, K_AA becomes NaN, and that NaN propagates into the integration-limit logic: valid_K_limit = min(K_AA, K_95) keeps NaN (min(nan, x)=nan when nan is first), the clamp min(max(nan,7),150)=nan, 10**log10(K_AA)=nan, and np.searchsorted(K, nan, 'right') returns len(K) — integrating the ENTIRE spectrum past the resolved range. Confirmed via _variance_method: with NaN K_AA and a spectrum whose polyfit minimum is rejected, n_var = len(K) (full range) instead of the K_95/K_AA-clamped range, producing a finite but over-integrated (biased-high) epsilon rather than a clean NaN. The temp/viscosity path is already guarded this way (l4.py:154-160), but the speed path is not.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Mirror the L3 floor in L4: replace l4.py:180 with `K_AA = 0.9 * f_AA / max(np.nan_to_num(W, nan=0.05), 0.05)` (and/or floor pspd_rel in the L3 output so L4 and the wavenumber axis are consistent). This turns a degenerate all-NaN-speed window into a clean, floored estimate instead of a silently wrong finite epsilon.

round1_2_raw.title: compare_l2 raises ValueError on empty section arrays (.max() on zero-size)
round1_2_raw.evidence: The 'section' dict is built unconditionally with int(ref_sec.max()) and int(comp_sec.max()) at compare.py:51-52. np.ndarray.max() raises ValueError('zero-size array to reduction operation maximum which has no identity') when section_number is empty (0 samples) — verified at runtime. process_l2 can return an L2 with zero-length arrays for a degenerate/empty L1 input, and the speed/shear/vib blocks below ARE guarded by both_in.any() but the section dict that runs first is not. A zero-sample comparison crashes the whole report instead of reporting 'no sections'.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Guard the reductions, e.g. `ref_n = int(ref_sec.max()) if ref_sec.size else 0` (and likewise for comp_sec), or early-return a minimal results dict when either section_number is empty.

round1_2_raw.title: L4Data.fom docstring mislabels the benchmark FOM as the obs/Nasmyth variance ratio
round1_2_raw.evidence: io.py:198 comments `fom` as 'figure of merit (obs/Nasmyth variance ratio)', but _read_l4 (io.py:666) loads it from the benchmark's FOM variable, whose long_name is 'The MAD of the spectrum relative to the 97.5 percentile of the expected MAD ...' (verified by reading the NetCDF attribute) — i.e. the Lueck (2022) MAD-normalized statistic, NOT the variance ratio. compare_l4 (compare.py:350-354) correctly compares computed.FM (the MAD-normalized statistic) against reference.fom, confirming the data flow is right; only the field comment is wrong. The variance-ratio quantity is the separate computed.fom in L4Data, which adds to the confusion.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Change the io.py:198 comment for the reference field to note it holds the ATOMIX FOM = MAD / (97.5-percentile expected MAD) (the Lueck 2022 statistic), distinct from the computed obs/Nasmyth variance-ratio fom.

round1_2_raw.title: EPSI_FINAL fallback emits a value when every probe in a window is QC-flagged
round1_2_raw.evidence: _compute_epsi_final first averages probes with flags==0; if none pass, it falls back to the geometric mean of ALL finite probes (l4.py:774-778), so a window where every probe failed QC still receives a non-NaN EPSI_FINAL. The ATOMIX benchmarks examined never contain an all-probes-flagged window (verified: VMP250 has 0 such windows), so this path is not exercised by the reference and there is no benchmark evidence it is wrong. But the behavior silently re-includes data that all per-probe flags rejected, which can mislead downstream consumers that treat EPSI_FINAL as 'best clean estimate'. Confidence low because it may be an intentional 'always produce a number' policy.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Either document this fallback explicitly as intentional, or set EPSI_FINAL=NaN (and let consumers fall back to per-probe epsi) when no probe passes QC, so the combined value never silently averages over uniformly-flagged data.

round1_2_raw.title: Method-1 epsilon time reference is corrupted by tz-offset stripping, silently NaN-ing all chi for non-UTC instruments
round1_2_raw.evidence: When a per-file epsilon dataset is read back from NetCDF, xarray's decode_cf decodes the `t` coord to absolute datetime64 by HONORING the offset in `units = "seconds since <start_time>"`. _epsilon_ds_to_l4data then converts back to seconds via `start = np.datetime64(re.sub(r"(Z|[+-]\d{2}:?\d{2})$", "", start_str))`, which STRIPS the offset and reinterprets the local wall-clock time as the reference. The comment on line 386 ("numpy.datetime64 doesn't accept tz suffix") is factually wrong: `np.datetime64('2025-01-15T03:04:05+10:00')` returns `2025-01-14T17:04:05` (correctly normalized to UTC, only emitting a deprecation UserWarning). Because PFile.start_time is built with a real tzinfo from the header `timezone_min` word (p_file.py:326-341), a non-UTC deployment yields e.g. `+10:00`. I reproduced the result: decoded epsilon times that should be [0,10,20] s come out [-36000,-35990,-35980] s — a 10-hour (36000 s) shift. Since `l3_chi.time` (chi side) is genuine seconds-since-start with no tz involved, process_l4_chi_epsilon's nearest-neighbour match finds |epsi_times - chi_times| ~= 36000 s >> max_dt and returns None for EVERY window, so Method-1 chi is silently all-NaN. The ARCTERX file happens to be `+00:00` (offset zero, harmless), and every `_epsilon_ds_to_l4data` test uses a raw float `t` coord that xarray never decodes to datetime64, so the entire tz-strip branch (lines 383-390) is untested.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Do not strip the offset; let it normalize to UTC the same way decode_cf does. Replace `start = np.datetime64(re.sub(...))` with an offset-aware parse, e.g. `start = np.datetime64(pd.Timestamp(start_str))` (pandas honors the offset and converts to UTC without the numpy warning), or `np.datetime64(datetime.fromisoformat(start_str).astimezone(UTC).replace(tzinfo=None))`. Add a regression test that builds the epsilon dataset with a CF `t` coord using a non-UTC `units = "seconds since 2025-01-15T03:04:05+10:00"`, round-trips through NetCDF so xarray decodes it, and asserts the recovered L4Data.time matches chi-side seconds (and that process_l4_chi_epsilon still pairs windows).

round1_2_raw.title: _epsilon_ds_to_l4data reads `fom` (obs/Nasmyth ratio) but rsi epsilon also writes the correct `FM` statistic it never consults
round1_2_raw.evidence: The Method-1 multi-probe filter path (active for all standard rsi outputs, since `epsilonMean` is written ONLY in chi_io and never by the rsi pipeline — grep confirms `epsilonMean` appears only in src/odas_tpw/rsi/chi_io.py) reads `_probe_var("fom")` and rejects probes with `fom > _EPS_FOM_LIMIT (1.15)`. The prior audit flagged that 1.15 is the FM limit, not a fom limit. The deeper, still-unaddressed half: dissipation.py writes BOTH `fom` (long_name 'observed/Nasmyth variance ratio', good ~1.0) AND `FM` (Lueck-2022 MAD statistic, good ~0, reject >1.15) as separate per-probe variables (dissipation.py:455-481), but chi_io reads only `fom`/`mad` and never `FM`. So even a one-line variable-name fix is insufficient context-free: the right QC field (`FM`) is present in the file but ignored.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: In _epsilon_ds_to_l4data, read `_probe_var("FM")` and filter `bad = ~np.isfinite(FM) | (FM > _EPS_FOM_LIMIT)` (FM good-fits approach 0). Keep `fom`/`mad` only for populating the L4Data diagnostic fields. Rename `_EPS_FOM_LIMIT` to `_EPS_FM_LIMIT` to reflect the Lueck statistic it gates.

round1_2_raw.title: load_epsilon_dataset concatenates a stale single-profile `_eps.nc` together with per-profile `_prof00N_eps.nc`, double-counting epsilon times
round1_2_raw.evidence: write_profile_results (helpers.py:473-477) names output `{stem}_eps.nc` when a file yields exactly one profile, but `{stem}_prof001_eps.nc`... when it yields several — a given run produces one form OR the other, never both. load_epsilon_dataset globs `{stem}_prof*_eps.nc` AND unconditionally inserts `single = {stem}_eps.nc` at index 0 if it exists (lines 858-861), then `xr.concat(datasets, dim="time", combine_attrs="override")`. If the output dir accumulates products from two runs whose profile detection disagreed (e.g. a re-run with different trim/direction params now finds 3 profiles where an earlier run found 1), both `{stem}_eps.nc` and `{stem}_prof00N_eps.nc` coexist and are concatenated, producing duplicated/overlapping epsilon time samples that silently bias the Method-1 nearest-neighbour epsilon. There is no overlap/duplicate-time guard.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Treat the single-profile and per-profile naming schemes as mutually exclusive: prefer the per-profile files when any `{stem}_prof*_eps.nc` exist and ignore `{stem}_eps.nc` in that case (or assert no time overlap before concatenating). Alternatively, after concat, drop duplicate `time` values (`ds = ds.sortby('time'); ds = ds.isel(time=np.unique(ds.time, return_index=True)[1])`) and warn.

round1_2_raw.title: Degenerate/duplicate epsilon window times collapse max_dt to 0, silently dropping all Method-1 chi
round1_2_raw.evidence: process_l4_chi_epsilon computes `max_dt = float(np.median(np.diff(np.sort(epsi_times)))) if len(epsi_times) > 1 else 30.0`. If two or more epsilon windows share an identical timestamp (degenerate/duplicate times — which the load_epsilon_dataset double-concat above can produce), `np.diff(np.sort(...))` contains zeros and the median can be exactly 0.0. Then the matcher `abs(epsi_times[idx_eps] - chi_times[j]) > max_dt` rejects every chi window whose time is not bit-exactly equal to an epsilon time, NaN-ing all Method-1 chi with no warning. Reproduced: median diff of [100,100,100] is 0.0.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Floor max_dt to a strictly positive value, e.g. `max_dt = max(float(np.median(np.diff(np.sort(np.unique(epsi_times))))), <one nominal window>)`, computing the median over unique times and guarding against a zero/NaN result before using it as a rejection threshold.

round1_2_raw.title: np.arange bin-edge construction produces a spurious extra trailing bin and reassigns the deepest boundary sample for non-binary-exact bin widths
round1_2_raw.evidence: bin_by_depth builds the grid as `d_min=np.floor(g_min/bw)*bw; d_max=np.ceil(g_max/bw)*bw; bin_edges=np.arange(d_min, d_max+bw, bw)`. When (d_max-d_min)/bw is an exact integer but bw is not binary-representable, np.arange's float accumulation makes the penultimate value land just below the `d_max+bw` stop, so an extra edge is emitted past d_max. Reproduced end-to-end: depths 0..7 m at bin_width=0.7 yield 11 bins (edges 0,0.7,...,7.0,7.7) instead of the intended 10 (edges 0..7.0). The spurious top bin [7.0,7.7) is mostly empty, AND the deepest sample at depth==7.0 (which under the correct 10-bin grid would hit the `coords==bin_edges[-1]` clamp and land in the last real bin [6.3,7.0)) instead becomes an interior-edge sample assigned to the spurious bin [7.0,7.7) — biasing both the intended deepest bin (loses its boundary sample) and adding a half-empty NaN-ish trailing bin to the published grid. Frequency over a depth sweep: bw=0.7 -> 160/1619 cases off-by-one; bw=0.1 -> 302/1619. Binary-exact widths (the defaults 1.0 m and 0.5 s, plus 0.25/2.0/5.0) never trigger it: 0/1619. The identical pattern is in bin_by_time (line 576) on epoch-second time grids. Tests use only binary-exact depth widths so the defect is uncovered (tests/test_perturb_binning.py bin widths: 1.0/2.0/5.0/10.0/20.0/100.0; the lone 0.1 is a time test that only checks variable presence, not bin count/edges).
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Compute the bin count integer-first and build edges with np.linspace (or a count-bounded arange) to avoid float-stop drift, e.g. `n_bins = int(round((d_max - d_min) / bin_width)); bin_edges = d_min + np.arange(n_bins + 1) * bin_width`. Apply the same fix in both bin_by_depth (line 442) and bin_by_time (line 576). Add a regression test at bin_width=0.7 (depths 0..7) asserting result.sizes['bin']==10 and that the deepest sample lands in the last real bin.

round1_2_raw.title: Duplicate time-bin coalescing in make_combo silently drops one file's data and is non-deterministic under np.argsort's default (non-stable) quicksort
round1_2_raw.evidence: For time combos, `order = np.argsort(tk)` then `keep[1:] &= tk[1:] != tk[:-1]` keeps the FIRST of any exact-duplicate timestamp after sorting and discards the whole duplicate row (data values included) via `combo.isel(time=np.flatnonzero(keep))` — no averaging/merge. Two issues: (1) np.argsort defaults to non-stable quicksort, so when two per-file binned datasets contribute the identical time-bin center, which file's row survives the tie is non-deterministic across numpy versions/pivots (verified: default kind is quicksort). (2) The surviving choice is arbitrary and the dropped row's binned values are silently lost rather than combined. In the standard pipeline each CTD file gets its own grid starting at its own t_min so exact bin-center collisions across files are rare, which keeps real-world impact low — but overlapping wall-clock windows that happen to align can hit it, and the loss is only logged as a count, not which samples.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Pass `kind='stable'` to np.argsort so tie order is deterministic (`order = np.argsort(tk, kind='stable')`). If silently discarding overlapping-window data is not desired, average/merge duplicate-time rows (e.g. groupby('time').mean()) instead of keep-first, or at minimum document that keep-first is intentional and the data are assumed non-overlapping.

round1_2_raw.title: csd_matrix overlap<0 clamps to nfft//2 (50% overlap) where MATLAB clamps to 0 (no overlap)
round1_2_raw.evidence: csd_matrix lines 197-200: `if overlap < 0 or overlap > 0.9 * nfft: overlap = nfft // 2`. The MATLAB reference csd_matrix_odas.m:132-136 handles the two cases differently: `if over_lap < 0, over_lap = 0; elseif over_lap > n_fft*0.9, over_lap = round(n_fft/2)`. So for a negative overlap MATLAB falls back to 0 (no overlap / non-overlapping segments), while the Python port silently substitutes 50% overlap. Verified numerically: overlap=-1 -> MATLAB 0, Python 128 (for nfft=256). This changes the segment count and the ensemble average. The same divergence exists in csd_matrix_batch (line 364: `if overlap is None or overlap < 0 or overlap > 0.9 * nfft: overlap = nfft // 2`). Low reachability: all internal callers (l3.py:148, l3_chi.py:141, window.py:365, goodman.py:150/238) pass `overlap = nfft // 2`, so a negative overlap never occurs in the pipeline; this only bites an external API user. The over-0.9*nfft branch already matches MATLAB (both -> nfft/2).
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Split the clamp to mirror MATLAB exactly: `if overlap is not None and overlap < 0: overlap = 0` then `elif overlap is None or overlap > 0.9 * nfft: overlap = nfft // 2`. Apply the same change in both csd_matrix (line 197) and csd_matrix_batch (line 364).

round1_2_raw.title: csd_matrix/clean_shear_spec auto-transpose `shape[0] < shape[1]` flips legitimately wide matrices, unlike MATLAB's isrow-only force
round1_2_raw.evidence: csd_matrix lines 180-181 transpose x whenever `x.shape[0] < x.shape[1]` (and 189-190 for y); goodman.py:138-141 does the same for accel/shear. The MATLAB references only force *row vectors* to columns via isrow() (csd_matrix_odas.m:99-100 `if isrow(x), x = x'`; clean_shear_spec.m:74-75 `if isrow(A), A = A'`), i.e. MATLAB transposes only the 1xN vector case, never a genuine wide MxN matrix with M<N. The Python heuristic silently transposes any input with fewer rows than columns, so an (n_channels, n_samples) matrix where n_samples happens to be < n_channels, or a short window with more channels than samples, would be silently and wrongly transposed instead of raising. Not reachable in the current pipeline (windows always have n_samples >= 2*nfft >> n_channels, and the 2*nfft length check on line 182 catches most misuse after the transpose), so impact is confined to external/edge use.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Restrict the auto-transpose to the true 1-D/row-vector case, matching MATLAB's isrow semantics: only transpose when one dimension is 1 (e.g. `if x.ndim == 2 and x.shape[0] == 1: x = x.T`). For genuine 2-D matrices, require samples-major orientation and raise a clear error if shape[0] < 2*nfft rather than silently transposing.

round1_2_raw.title: despike.py filtfilt uses scipy default padlen (6), not MATLAB convention (3) — port divergence the codebase already fixed elsewhere
round1_2_raw.evidence: Both filtfilt calls in _single_despike omit the padlen argument: `dv_hp = np.abs(filtfilt(b_hp, a_hp, dv_padded))` (line 110) and `dv_lp = filtfilt(b_lp, a_lp, dv_hp)` (line 114). scipy defaults to padlen = 3*max(len(a),len(b)) = 6 for these 1st-order Butterworth filters, whereas MATLAB's filtfilt (used by despike.m lines 184, 192) uses padlen = 3*(max(len(a),len(b))-1) = 3. The codebase explicitly documents and corrects exactly this divergence in the SAME subpackage: src/odas_tpw/scor160/l2.py:15-25 `_matlab_padlen()` notes 'scipy defaults to 3*nfilt ... For a 1st-order Butterworth (nfilt=2) this is padlen=3 vs scipy's 6 — enough to cause ~1% vibration RMS error', and applies it to its own filtfilt calls (l2.py:198,277,283). despike.py was not given the same treatment. Concrete consequence verified: for a length-2 input despike crashes with `ValueError: The length of the input vector x must be greater than padlen, which is 6` (padded length = 2 + 2*2 = 6, not > 6), whereas MATLAB despike runs (padded 6 > 3). Numeric impact on retained signal is negligible in normal operation because despike's own explicit reflect-padding (pad_len ~2048) dominates, but the inconsistency with the rest of the subpackage and the short-array crash are real.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Import/replicate `_matlab_padlen` and pass `padlen=_matlab_padlen(b_hp, a_hp)` and `padlen=_matlab_padlen(b_lp, a_lp)` to the two filtfilt calls so despike matches MATLAB's edge-transient handling and the convention already used by l2.py. This also lets length-2 sections run instead of crashing.

round1_2_raw.title: despike.py silently replaces data with 0.0 when a bad block spans the whole (padded) array — diverges from MATLAB (NaN) and hides data loss
round1_2_raw.evidence: When both the before-region and after-region of a bad block contain no valid points, the replacement falls through to `replacement = 0.0` (line 164) and writes it into the signal (line 166). MATLAB despike.m unconditionally computes `dv(start:stop) = (start_value + stop_value)/2` where empty regions give `sum([])/length([]) = 0/0 = NaN` (lines 235-245), so MATLAB yields NaN, not 0.0. Verified reachable: `despike(np.array of length 5 with a spike at index 2, fs=512)` marks the entire padded array bad (bad window s±2N_half clamps to [0,len]), hits the 0.0 branch, and returns a signal where the spike location is now exactly 0.0; for length 10 the reported fraction is 1.0 (whole signal replaced). Both MATLAB-NaN and Python-0.0 are pathological, but Python's silent 0.0 is worse because it masks total data loss (a finite, plausible-looking value) and the divergence is a port-faithfulness break. Only reachable when the removal width N exceeds the section length, so unreachable through the L2 pipeline (which enforces min_duration), but reachable via direct API use or a misconfigured large N.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: When neither side has valid points, return NaN (to match MATLAB and to make the data loss visible to downstream QC) rather than 0.0; alternatively raise/skip when the bad block covers the entire array. Add a regression test on a short array exercising this branch.

round1_2_raw.title: despike.py raises bare ValueError (no guard) for inputs of length <= 2
round1_2_raw.evidence: For length-1 or length-2 inputs, the first filtfilt call (line 110) raises `ValueError: The length of the input vector x must be greater than padlen, which is 6` because the reflect-padded length (3*L for L < 2*int(fs/smooth)) does not exceed scipy's padlen=6. There is no length guard in `despike()` or `_single_despike()`; the exception propagates uncaught. Tested: lengths 1 and 2 both raise; length 3 works. A degenerate 1-2 sample segment carries no useful information so erroring is defensible, but a bare scipy ValueError is an unhelpful failure mode for a public API function.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Add an early-return guard at the top of `despike()`: if the input is too short to filter (e.g. len(y) < 3, or len < the effective padlen), return the input unchanged with empty spike_indices, n_passes=0, fraction=0.0. Lowering padlen per the first finding also resolves the length-2 case.

round1_2_raw.title: despike.py spike-removal window uses N//2 (floor) where MATLAB uses round(N/2), giving a ~1-sample window difference for odd N
round1_2_raw.evidence: Line 93 sets `N_half = N // 2` (integer floor) and the bad window is `good[max(0,s-N_half) : min(len, s+2*N_half+1)]` (lines 131-132). MATLAB despike.m sets local `N = N/2` (float) then `idx = round(max(1,s-N) : min(L, s+2*N))` (lines 166, 217), applying round() to a non-integer half-width. For odd user-supplied N the windows differ by ~1 sample on each side (e.g. N=15: MATLAB removes s-8..s+15 after rounding 7.5; Python removes s-7..s+14). The default N=round(0.04*fs)=20 for fs=512 is even, so the default path matches exactly; callers in l2.py compute `N = round(sh_hw*fs)` which can be odd. Physical impact is sub-sample (one extra/fewer point at a spike boundary) and negligible, but it is a genuine port divergence.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: If exact MATLAB parity for odd N is desired, compute the half-width as a float (`N_half = N/2`) and round the resulting boundary indices (`round(s - N_half)`, `round(s + 2*N_half)`) instead of pre-flooring N to N_half. Otherwise, document the floor-vs-round difference. Low priority given the negligible physical effect and the even default.

round1_2_raw.title: gradT_noise / gradT_noise_batch / fp07_tau drop the MATLAB speed>0 validation, yielding negative noise, inf, or NaN for invalid speed
round1_2_raw.evidence: The MATLAB reference gradT_noise_odas.m line 157 explicitly validates speed via `val_speed = @(x) isnumeric(x) && all(x) > 0`. The Python port drops this guard. Verified directly: `gradT_noise([10,20], 10.0, -0.7)` returns negative noise `[-2.5e-08, -6.9e-08]` (physically impossible — a noise PSD must be >=0); `speed=0.0` returns `[inf, inf]`; and `fp07_tau(-0.7, 'lueck')` returns NaN from `(1/speed)**0.5`. These are public API functions (exported in chi/__init__.py and called directly from rsi/window.py and rsi/quick_look.py). The live pipeline happens to be safe because both entry points floor speed upstream (rsi/window.py:100 `W = float(np.mean(np.abs(speed)))` then clamp to SPEED_MIN; chi/l3_chi.py:157 `np.maximum(nan_to_num(nanmean(np.abs(...))), 0.01)`), so this is a latent/defense-in-depth gap, not a live data-corruption bug.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Add a guard at the top of gradT_noise / gradT_noise_batch / fp07_tau, e.g. `speed = np.abs(np.asarray(speed, float)); speed = np.maximum(speed, 1e-3)` (or raise ValueError for speed<=0 to mirror MATLAB), so the standalone library functions cannot emit negative/inf/NaN noise when called outside the guarded pipeline.

round1_2_raw.title: Broken-thermistor R_ratio clamp warns in scalar noise_thermchannel but is silent in noise_thermchannel_batch
round1_2_raw.evidence: Scalar noise_thermchannel (lines 435-441) emits `warnings.warn(f"R_ratio={...} < 0.1 (possible broken thermistor ...); clamped to 1.0")` when R_ratio < 0.1. The vectorized noise_thermchannel_batch (line 574) performs the identical clamp `R_ratio = np.where(R_ratio < 0.1, 1.0, R_ratio)` but emits NO warning. Since the batch path (chi/l3_chi.py:223 gradT_noise_batch) is the main production pipeline, a broken/abnormal thermistor T_mean that triggers the clamp is silently swallowed there, while the same condition is surfaced in the scalar path. Both produce numerically identical output (verified: scalar==batch at the clamped T), so this is purely a diagnostic-visibility inconsistency, not a numerical defect.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: In noise_thermchannel_batch, after the np.where clamp, check `if np.any(R_ratio_pre < 0.1): warnings.warn(...)` reporting how many T_means were clamped, mirroring the scalar version, so broken-thermistor windows are not silently masked in the batch pipeline.

round1_2_raw.title: PFile._read() lacks short-header length guard, raising raw struct.error on truncated files
round1_2_raw.evidence: `_read()` does `raw_hdr = f.read(HEADER_BYTES)` then immediately calls `_detect_endian(raw_hdr, ...)` / `_parse_header(...)` with no length check. If the file is shorter than 128 bytes, `struct.unpack_from`/`struct.unpack` raise `struct.error` (NOT ValueError). Reproduced: `PFile` on a 32-byte file raises `struct.error: unpack_from requires a buffer of at least 128 bytes`. Notably, the sibling debug utility `extract_pfile_segment` (lines 128-130) DOES guard this exact case with `if len(raw_hdr) < HEADER_BYTES: raise ValueError(...)`, so the defensive precedent already exists but was not applied to the primary reader. Because `struct.error` is not a subclass of OSError/ValueError/RuntimeError, it escapes every downstream batch handler (see convert_all and cli.py), turning one corrupt file into a whole-batch crash.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: After `raw_hdr = f.read(HEADER_BYTES)` in `_read()`, add `if len(raw_hdr) < HEADER_BYTES: raise ValueError(f"{self.filepath.name}: file too small for header")`, mirroring extract_pfile_segment. This converts the failure to the ValueError that all callers already catch.

round1_2_raw.title: convert_all aborts entire batch on struct.error from a corrupt .p file
round1_2_raw.evidence: Both the serial loop (`except (OSError, ValueError, RuntimeError) as e` at line 605) and the parallel loop (`future.result()` then same except at line 616) are explicitly designed to log-and-continue per file. But `PFile` on a sub-128-byte/truncated header raises `struct.error`, which is a subclass only of `Exception`, not of the three caught types. Reproduced: `convert_all([tiny_corrupt_file], out, jobs=1)` raises `struct.error` and aborts instead of logging and moving on.
