The CLI `nc` subcommand (cli.py:223) and its wrapper `except (FileNotFoundError, OSError, ValueError)` (cli.py:253) also miss `struct.error`, so `rsi-tpw nc VMP/*.p` crashes with a traceback if any file is corrupt.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Primary fix: guard the short header in PFile._read() (finding above) so a ValueError is raised. Defensively, also broaden the except clauses here to include struct.error, e.g. `except (OSError, ValueError, RuntimeError, struct.error) as e` (import struct), or catch `Exception` in these batch log-and-continue loops.

round1_2_raw.title: Corrupt record_size/n_cols produces cryptic 'cannot reshape array' crash instead of a clear diagnostic
round1_2_raw.evidence: `_read()` computes `data_words = (record_size - header_size) // 2`, `scans_per_record = data_words // self.n_cols`, `total_scans = n_records * scans_per_record`, then `raw_flat = records['data'].reshape(total_scans, self.n_cols)`. When a corrupt header makes `data_words` not an integer multiple of `n_cols`, `records['data']` has `n_records*data_words` elements but the target shape requests `total_scans*n_cols` (smaller), so reshape raises `ValueError: cannot reshape array of size N into shape (...)`. Reproduced via a synthetic file with fast_cols=3, record_size=256 (data_words=64, 64%3!=0). Unlike `extract_pfile_segment` (lines 142-148), `_read()` performs NO validation of record_size/header_size/config_size before use, so the failure surfaces as an opaque numpy reshape error rather than 'corrupt record_size'.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Before the reshape, validate header geometry in _read() as extract_pfile_segment already does: check `record_size > header_size >= HEADER_BYTES`, `config_size >= 0`, and that `(record_size - header_size) // 2` is a positive multiple of `n_cols`; raise a descriptive ValueError otherwise.

round1_2_raw.title: Zero n_cols or n_rows in a corrupt header causes bare ZeroDivisionError
round1_2_raw.evidence: `self.fs_fast = f_clock / self.n_cols` (line 322) and `self.fs_slow = self.fs_fast / self.n_rows` (line 323) use header-derived `n_cols = fast_cols + slow_cols` and `n_rows` without validating they are non-zero. Reproduced: a synthetic file with fast_cols=slow_cols=0 raises a bare `ZeroDivisionError: division by zero`; likewise n_rows=0. No file path/context is attached, making batch triage hard. extract_pfile_segment validates its size fields but the sampling-rate divisors here are unchecked.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Validate `n_cols >= 1` and `n_rows >= 1` (and `f_clock > 0`) right after reading them, raising `ValueError(f"{self.filepath.name}: invalid matrix geometry n_cols={n_cols} n_rows={n_rows}")`.

round1_2_raw.title: GRADT temperature-gradient computation crashes on a single-sample fast channel
round1_2_raw.evidence: For each fast FP07 channel: `dTdt = np.diff(T_fast_ch) * pf.fs_fast; dTdt = np.append(dTdt, dTdt[-1])`. If a fast channel has exactly one sample (a degenerate 1-scan file), `np.diff` returns an empty array and `dTdt[-1]` raises `IndexError: index -1 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 0`. Reproduced in isolation. Reachable only for a pathological ~1-scan file (PFile already enforces n_records>=1, so this needs a single-scan record), hence nit severity.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Guard the degenerate case, e.g. `if T_fast_ch.size < 2: dTdt = np.zeros_like(T_fast_ch)` else compute as now; or skip GRADT when len(t_fast) < 2.

round1_2_raw.title: Corrupt matrix row / channel id in config string raises uncaught ValueError during PFile parse
round1_2_raw.evidence: parse_config line 249 does `[int(x) for x in val.split()]` for matrix rows, and _read line 390 does `[int(x) for x in ch['id'].replace(',',' ').split()]`. A corrupt/garbage config (e.g. `row1 = 1 abc 3`, or `id = 1 foo`) raises a bare `ValueError: invalid literal for int()` that propagates out of PFile.__init__. Reproduced for the matrix case via parse_config('[matrix]\nrow1 = 1 abc 3\n'). The config string is embedded untrusted data; the failure is a ValueError (which most batch loops catch) but the message lacks file/line context.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Wrap the int() conversions with a try/except that re-raises a contextual ValueError naming the file and the offending row/id token, so batch processing reports which file/section is malformed.

round1_2_raw.title: Joined 32-bit (2-id) channels never get ODAS's signed-by-default correction
round1_2_raw.evidence: For 2-id channels the port computes `odd_data * 2**16 + even_data` (always unsigned/positive, lines 471-473) and the later sign block (503-523) only ever performs the UNSIGNED wrap; there is no `value >= 2**31 -> value - 2**32` path anywhere in p_file.py (grep confirms no 2**31/2**32 occurrences except timezone/int16). ODAS read_odas.m (lines 388-398) treats every joined 32-bit channel as SIGNED by default unless `sign=unsigned`, applying `ch(ch>=2^31) -= 2^32`. So a 2-id channel of a non-skipped type with the high bit set would differ from ODAS by 2^32. Mitigating fact (why low confidence): the only 2-id channel in the real ARCTERX/SN479 data is JAC_C (type jac_c), which ODAS explicitly SKIPS in its sign loop (read_odas.m:362-368), keeping it unsigned-joined — matching the Python behavior. The divergence is latent unless a future config declares a signed raw 32-bit channel (as the unit test test_two_id_channel_decoded does with type=raw).
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: After joining 2-id channels, replicate ODAS default-signed behavior: for joined channels whose type is not in the ODAS skip set (sbt/sbc/jac_c/o2_43f) and not marked sign=unsigned, apply `ch[ch >= 2**31] -= 2**32`. At minimum add a comment documenting the intentional unsigned-only handling and that it is only safe because real 2-id channels are jac_c.

round1_2_raw.title: Bottom-crash trim silently skipped when detected crash depth is at/above the segment's first retained slow sample (caller off-by-one, aggravated by PR #78 M4)
round1_2_raw.evidence: Caller: `below = np.where(P_seg >= bottom_depth)[0]; candidate = new_s + int(below[0]) - 1; if candidate > new_s: new_e = candidate`. When the crash depth is <= P_slow[new_s] (whole remaining segment lies below the crash), `below[0]==0`, so `candidate = new_s - 1`, which fails the `candidate > new_s` guard. The crash is then NOT trimmed at all — the entire below-crash segment is retained. Reproduced: P_slow=[40,42,44,46,48], bottom_depth=39 -> below[0]=0 -> candidate=-1 -> no trim. PR #78's M4 change in processing/bottom.py now returns the flagged-bin SAMPLE MEAN (shallower than the bin center it previously returned), which marginally increases the chance that the reported depth falls at/above P_slow[new_s] and triggers this no-trim path. The off-by-one predates PR #78 (introduced in PR #8), but the M4 fix interacts with it.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Handle the `below[0]==0` case explicitly: if the entire segment is at/below the crash depth, the crash starts at or before new_s, so the segment should be dropped or trimmed to new_s (e.g. `candidate = new_s + int(below[0]) - 1; new_e = max(candidate, new_s)` only when below[0]>0, and treat below[0]==0 as 'crash encompasses the whole tail' -> set new_e=new_s or flag the profile). At minimum, log a warning so the un-trimmed-crash case is visible rather than silent.

round1_2_raw.title: Bottom-crash sample-mean can under-trim by up to half a bin width when the crash onset is early in the flagged bin
round1_2_raw.evidence: PR #78 M4 returns `float(np.nanmean(sel_depth))` — the mean depth of the flagged bin's samples. This guarantees the reported depth is <= the deepest sample (fixing the prior 'matches nothing' bug), but when the crash onset is early in the bin the mean lies BELOW the onset, leaving contaminated data un-trimmed. Reproduced with default depth_window=4m: crash onset at 46.2m in deepest bin [46,50), profile reaching 49.5m -> reported depth 47.75m -> 1.55m of crash data (46.2-47.75) kept above the reported depth. The trim is conservative in the over-shoot direction but under-trims relative to the crash onset by up to ~half a bin (~2m at the default window).
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: This is a fundamental limit of the 4m bin resolution; the mean is a defensible compromise (the prior center/edge had the same granularity plus the catastrophic match-nothing failure). If tighter bottom trimming matters, report the bin's MINIMUM (shallowest) flagged-sample depth, or sub-bin the flagged bin to locate the accel-magnitude rise edge. Otherwise document the ~half-bin under-trim in the docstring so callers can size depth_window accordingly.

round1_2_raw.title: chi variance-correction grid is fixed at 80000 points per call regardless of n_fine, ~40x the prior size
round1_2_raw.evidence: PR #78 M1 sets `n = max(int(n_fine), _K_SPAN_KB * _PTS_PER_KB)` = max(2000, 40*2000) = 80000 always. `_variance_correction` is called once per fit/iteration per window (chi.py:261, 440, 588), each building two 80000-element float64 arrays plus a grad_func evaluation. Measured ~0.96 ms/call vs. the prior 2000-point grid. The commit's own verification showed <0.05% error at 2000 points-per-kB; the peak only needs ~few hundred points-per-kB to resolve, so _PTS_PER_KB=2000 over-resolves.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Lower `_PTS_PER_KB` (e.g. 200-500) after confirming accuracy stays <0.1% against the high-n reference, or cap n with an upper bound. The correctness of M1 is fine; this is purely the cost of the chosen resolution.

round1_2_raw.title: top_trim silently skips length-mismatched channels instead of aborting, inconsistent with bottom-crash
round1_2_raw.evidence: In compute_trim_depth, a channel whose length != len(depth) is silently `continue`d (line 82), so the trim is computed from whatever channels happen to match. detect_bottom_crash takes the opposite stance — any length mismatch returns None and aborts the whole detection (bottom.py:82-83). A caller mistake (passing a channel sliced to the wrong rate) thus silently degrades top_trim to fewer channels with no warning, but aborts bottom-crash. Neither is wrong, but the inconsistency can mask a wiring bug.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Make the two helpers consistent: either both log a warning on a length mismatch (preferred — the caller can see the dropped channel), or both abort. A `logging.warning("top_trim: channel %s length %d != depth %d, skipping", name, len(data), len(depth))` at the skip site would surface the misuse.

round1_2_raw.title: compute_trim_depth returns the SHALLOWEST sub-threshold bin, not the prop-wash exit: a momentarily-quiet top bin causes severe under-trim that leaves the noisy near-surface section in the profile
round1_2_raw.evidence: The per-channel loop `for i in range(len(std_arr)): if isfinite(std_arr[i]) and std_arr[i] < threshold: trim_depths.append(bin_centers[i]); break` returns the FIRST (shallowest) bin whose std drops below the channel's own quantile threshold. The module docstring (processing/__init__.py:9-10) states the intent is 'depth where shear/acceleration variance settles below a quantile threshold (prop-wash exit)' i.e. the depth past which the profile is settled. But on a real cast the very top bin is frequently momentarily quiet (instrument just released, before the prop-wash / instability ramps up). I verified: with noise injected only over 5-15 m and a quiet bin [0.5,1.5), `compute_trim_depth(depth, {'sh': sh}, dz=1.0, min_depth=1.0, max_depth=50.0, quantile=0.6)` returns 1.0 m, leaving the entire 5-15 m noisy section IN the profile. The caller (perturb/pipeline.py:398 `above = np.where(P_seg >= trim_depth)[0]`) faithfully pushes the start to ~1 m, propagating the under-trim into the trimmed cast. Compounding the problem, `threshold` is the 60th percentile of that channel's OWN std distribution INCLUDING the noisy top bins (line 95), which inflates the threshold and makes an early quiet bin even more likely to qualify. The correct 'exit' semantic is the deepest contiguous exceedance (scan from the top for the LAST bin >= threshold, then trim just below it), or scanning top-down for the first SUSTAINED quiet region.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Change the search to find the prop-wash EXIT, not the first quiet sample: scan from the top and record the deepest bin that still EXCEEDS the threshold, then return the next bin center below it (e.g. `last_noisy = max(i for i in range(len(std_arr)) if isfinite(std_arr[i]) and std_arr[i] >= threshold); trim_depths.append(bin_centers[last_noisy+1])`), guarding the end of the array. Add a regression test with a quiet-top / noisy-middle / quiet-bottom profile asserting the trim depth lands below the noisy section, not at min_depth.

round1_2_raw.title: detect_bottom_crash emits a spurious 'All-NaN slice encountered' RuntimeWarning on the all-NaN-depth path it explicitly intends to handle silently (np.errstate does not suppress nanmax's warning)
round1_2_raw.evidence: Lines 89-92 wrap `max_depth = np.nanmax(depth)` in `with np.errstate(invalid='ignore'):` with the comment 'An all-NaN depth segment makes nanmax NaN ... Bail out cleanly instead of crashing the profile.' But `np.errstate(invalid='ignore')` only suppresses IEEE floating-point error flags; the 'All-NaN slice encountered' message is raised via `warnings.warn(RuntimeWarning)`, which errstate does NOT intercept. I confirmed against the production path: `detect_bottom_crash(np.full(1000, np.nan), {'Ax': np.random.randn(1000)}, fs=512.0)` raises RuntimeWarning under `-W error::RuntimeWarning`, and the existing test `test_all_nan_depth_returns_none` already trips it (pytest reports the warning). All-NaN depth segments are a legitimately-occurring case (e.g. a profile where P_fast is fully masked), so this pollutes logs / can be escalated to an error by callers that promote warnings.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Suppress the actual warning category instead of (or in addition to) the FP errstate, e.g. `with warnings.catch_warnings(): warnings.simplefilter('ignore', RuntimeWarning); max_depth = np.nanmax(depth)` — or short-circuit before nanmax with `if not np.any(np.isfinite(depth)): return None`. Same pattern should be checked anywhere `np.nanmax/nanmedian` is guarded only by `np.errstate`.

round1_2_raw.title: Undeclared `pandas` runtime dependency crashes documented GPS-CSV and hotel-CSV/ISO ingestion on a clean install
round1_2_raw.evidence: pandas is imported and used unconditionally with no ImportError guard in reachable, config-selected code paths, yet it is absent from pyproject.toml `dependencies` (numpy/netCDF4/scipy/xarray/gsw/ruamel.yaml/matplotlib/cmocean only). gps.py:214 `import pandas as pd; df = pd.read_csv(file)` is hit whenever `gps.source: "csv"` (a documented option, config.py:326) via the factory at gps.py:359 (`return GPSFromCSV(config["file"], ...)`). hotel.py:285-287 `_load_csv` does the same `import pandas as pd; df = pd.read_csv(path)` and is dispatched by `load_hotel` for any `.csv` hotel file (hotel.py:268-269), which the perturb pipeline calls at pipeline.py:1884-1886. hotel.py:177 and :186 additionally hard-import pandas for `time_format='iso'` and the `auto`/non-numeric branch via `pd.to_datetime`. On a fresh `pip install microstructure-tpw`, all of these raise `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pandas'` for valid user configurations. Unlike the optional-dep pattern used elsewhere (pyturb/bin.py:62-67 wraps `import gsw` in try/except ImportError with a graceful fallback), none of these pandas imports are guarded.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Add `"pandas>=2.0,<3"` to `[project] dependencies` in pyproject.toml (pandas 2.x is the current series and is compatible with the numpy>=2.0 floor). If pandas is meant to be optional (only needed for CSV/ISO hotel and CSV GPS ingestion), instead wrap each `import pandas as pd` in try/except ImportError and raise a clear actionable error (e.g. 'CSV/ISO ingestion requires pandas; install microstructure-tpw[csv]') and declare it under a new optional-dependencies group. Either way the current state (hard import, undeclared) is a defect.

round1_2_raw.title: mypy per-module overrides reference three nonexistent modules (config drift), silently suppressing nothing
round1_2_raw.evidence: The `[[tool.mypy.overrides]]` `module` list includes `odas_tpw.rsi.spectral`, `odas_tpw.rsi.ocean`, and `odas_tpw.rsi.nasmyth` (pyproject.toml:65-67). None of these files exist under src/odas_tpw/rsi/ (verified: `find src/odas_tpw/rsi -name spectral* -o -name ocean* -o -name nasmyth*` returns nothing; no module imports `odas_tpw.rsi.spectral/ocean/nasmyth`; git log shows no deleted predecessors — the names appear to be leftovers from when these utilities were consolidated into scor160 (`odas_tpw.scor160.spectral/ocean/nasmyth`, which ARE present at lines 77-79). mypy silently ignores overrides for modules it never sees, so the `disable_error_code` block (operator/index/arg-type/etc.) is a no-op for these three entries. The drift is benign at runtime but misleads readers into thinking rsi.spectral/ocean/nasmyth exist and are intentionally lenient, and masks the fact that no real rsi module is getting that suppression by that name. Blame: added 2026-03-13 (b11117ae) and never updated after the move.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Delete the three stale lines `"odas_tpw.rsi.spectral",`, `"odas_tpw.rsi.ocean",`, `"odas_tpw.rsi.nasmyth",` from the override module list. If those error codes are still needed somewhere in rsi, add the correct current module name instead. Consider periodically validating override modules exist (a one-line script in CI) to prevent recurrence.

round1_2_raw.title: Deconvolution runs BEFORE the unsigned-wrap step, reversing ODAS order — corrupts any pre-emphasized unsigned channel (e.g. micro-conductivity C1_dC1) at the int16 wrap point
round1_2_raw.evidence: ODAS does sign/unsigned correction inside read_odas.m (called at odas_p2mat.m:372) FIRST, then deconvolves (odas_p2mat.m:526+). In read_odas.m the unsigned block runs `ch(ch<0)+=2^16` before any deconvolution. The Python _read() reverses this: _apply_deconvolution() is called at p_file.py:498, while the `sign=='unsigned'` wrap loop runs afterward at p_file.py:503-523. For a pre-emphasized channel whose raw int16 wraps past 32767 (high-amplitude unsigned sensors such as JAC/RSI micro-conductivity C1_dC1 — which carry diff_gain and are deconvolved), the deconvolve sees a sign discontinuity (e.g. 32767 -> -32768) that ODAS never sees. I reproduced this: deconvolving raw-signed-with-wrap vs unsigned-corrected-monotone data (counts 30000->40000) gives max abs difference 67655 = 6.8x the true signal range, because the wrap step is a huge spurious derivative the deconvolution amplifies. T1/T2/P in ARCTERX are signed so unaffected, but the order is latently wrong for any unsigned pre-emphasized channel.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Move the unsigned-wrap loop (p_file.py:503-523) to run BEFORE _apply_deconvolution() so deconvolve operates on sign-corrected data, matching ODAS read_odas->deconvolve ordering. Verify T1/T2/P (signed, no `sign` field) are unaffected by the reorder.

round1_2_raw.title: parse_config crashes with a bare ValueError on a non-integer matrix entry or channel id in a corrupt/truncated config string
round1_2_raw.evidence: parse_config line 249 does `result['matrix'].append([int(x) for x in val.split()])` and PFile._read line 390 does `ids=[int(x) for x in ch['id'].replace(',',' ').split()]`. A corrupted/garbled config (e.g. record-0 partially overwritten, or `errors='replace'` decode producing non-numeric tokens) raises a bare `ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'x'` with no file context. Verified: parse_config on a config with `row01 = 1 2 x 4` crashes with exactly that message. This is distinct from the already-confirmed header-size/n_cols crashes (those are binary-header fields; this is the ASCII config block, a different parse path).
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Wrap the int() conversions in try/except ValueError and re-raise with the filename and the offending key/line, e.g. `raise ValueError(f"{self.filepath.name}: malformed matrix row {key!r}: {val!r}") from exc`. Same for the channel id parse.

round1_2_raw.title: Deconvolve sign-inversion branch (p[0] < -0.5, which negates the entire pre-emphasized signal) has zero test coverage
round1_2_raw.evidence: deconvolve.py:66-68 mutates the signal: `if p[0] < -0.5: X_dX = -X_dX.astype(np.float64)` — the in-place sign correction ODAS applies for instruments whose derivative circuit is inverted (deconvolve.m:188 `if p(1)<-0.5, X_dX=-X_dX`). tests/test_deconvolve.py exercises only the normal (positive-correlation), X=None, empty, and interpolation paths; grep for `-0.5`/`invert`/`negat`/`p[0]` in the test file returns nothing. A regression here (e.g. dropping the int16->float64 promotion, or flipping the threshold) would silently produce sign-flipped temperature/pressure with no failing test. The promotion guard is load-bearing: -int16(-32768) wraps without it.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Add a test that builds X_dX anti-correlated with X (so polyfit slope < -0.5), includes an int16 -32768 sample, and asserts deconvolve recovers the correct (un-inverted) signal and stays finite — exercising both the threshold and the .astype(np.float64) promotion.

round1_2_raw.title: Deconvolve propagates a single NaN across the entire channel via lfilter with no guard (same failure class as the PR #78 M3 ct_align fix, left uncovered in this path)
round1_2_raw.evidence: If any sample of X or X_dX is non-finite, np.polyfit(X_interp, X_dX, 1) at line 65 returns NaN coefficients and scipy lfilter (lines 72, 82, 95) smears the NaN forward through every subsequent output, silently corrupting the whole deconvolved channel — exactly the smearing the PR #78 M3 ct_align fix guards against (it added `if not (np.all(np.isfinite(seg_T)) and np.all(np.isfinite(seg_C))): continue`). In the normal PFile flow deconvolve receives raw int16 counts (cannot be NaN), so the live risk is low; but deconvolve() is a public function (imported in tests and callable on float64 hres / hotel-file data), and unlike ct_align it has no finite-input guard. A documented defense-in-depth gap given M3 just established the pattern.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: At the top of deconvolve(), after the empty-array check, add `if not np.all(np.isfinite(X_dX)) or (X is not None and not np.all(np.isfinite(X))): warn and return X_dX-shaped NaN (or filter only finite span)`. Mirrors the M3 finite guard so the same poison cannot pass through the deconvolve path.

round1_2_raw.title: csd_matrix accepts a length-1/scalar custom window and silently broadcasts it to a rectangular window (no nfft-length validation)
round1_2_raw.evidence: When a caller passes an explicit window, the only handling is `window = ... if window is None else np.asarray(window, dtype=np.float64)` (line 201). There is no length check against nfft, unlike the MATLAB reference csd_matrix_odas.m lines 124-126 which errors if `size(Window,1) ~= n_fft`. The window is then applied via `_detrend_segment(...) * window` (line 214) / `... * window` (lines 235,237). A wrong-length window of length != {nfft,1} raises a numpy broadcast ValueError (verified), but a length-1 or 0-d scalar window broadcasts SILENTLY to a uniform rectangular window of that scale, producing a wrong (un-windowed) spectrum with no error (verified: `csd_matrix(x,None,256,100.0,window=np.array([2.0]))` returns shape (129,2,2) silently). All internal callers pass window=None, so this is an external-API hazard only.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: After `window = np.asarray(window, ...)`, validate `if window.ndim != 1 or window.shape[0] != nfft: raise ValueError(f"window must be a 1-D array of length nfft={nfft}, got shape {window.shape}")`, matching csd_matrix_odas.m's check. csd_odas/csd_matrix_batch inherit the fix (batch has no window param, so add the same check there only if a window param is ever exposed).

round1_2_raw.title: csd_matrix_batch omits the 2*nfft minimum-length requirement enforced by csd_matrix and the MATLAB reference (allows statistically-weak single-segment spectra)
round1_2_raw.evidence: The non-batch csd_matrix enforces `if x.shape[0] < 2*nfft: raise` (line 182-183), matching csd_matrix_odas.m line 110-111 (`size(x,1) < 2*n_fft -> error`). The batch path only checks `n_seg < 1` (line 372), i.e. `diss_length >= nfft` (for 0 overlap) or `>= ~nfft` for 50% overlap — so a window of length between nfft and 2*nfft yields a single FFT segment and passes, diverging from both csd_matrix and MATLAB which require >=2 segments for statistical significance. clean_shear_spec also requires 2*nfft via the wrapped csd_matrix, but clean_shear_spec_batch does not. In practice l3/l3_chi dissipation windows are many multiples of nfft, so this is not currently triggered; it is a faithfulness/robustness gap, not an active bug.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Add `if diss_length < 2*nfft: raise ValueError(f"diss_length ({diss_length}) must be at least 2*nfft ({2*nfft})")` after unpacking the shape (around line 359), mirroring csd_matrix and csd_matrix_odas.m, so the batch and non-batch paths agree on the minimum statistical length.

round1_2_raw.title: Cross-spectrum DC/Nyquist halving is symmetric in Python but asymmetric in MATLAB reference (deliberate divergence; 2 edge bins differ)
round1_2_raw.evidence: In the cross case, csd_matrix halves DC and Nyquist for all three matrices (`for arr in (Cxx,Cyy,Cxy): arr[0]/=2; arr[-1]/=2`, lines 247-250). The MATLAB csd_matrix_odas.m halves Cxy at both ends (lines 252-253) but Cxx only at DC (line 257: `Cxx(1)/2`, no Cxx(end)) and Cyy only at Nyquist (line 258: `Cyy(end)/2`, no Cyy(1)). So Python additionally halves Cxx's Nyquist bin and Cyy's DC bin where MATLAB leaves them un-halved. The Python behavior is the physically-correct/Parseval-consistent one and the MATLAB asymmetry is a known RSI inconsistency; these bins (DC of AA, Nyquist of UU in the Goodman path) are excluded from the dissipation integral (l4 qc_idx starts at 1 and integrates well below Nyquist), so the numerical impact is negligible. Flagging only for traceability of the intentional divergence.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: No code change needed; the symmetric halving is more correct. Add a one-line comment near lines 247-250 documenting that this deliberately corrects csd_matrix_odas.m's asymmetric Cxx/Cyy edge halving (Cxx(1)/2 and Cyy(end)/2 only), so future port-faithfulness reviews do not 'fix' it back to the MATLAB asymmetry.

round1_2_raw.title: Python Kraichnan gradient spectrum uses a different functional form than the in-repo MATLAB reference (and standard literature), biasing kB/epsilon_T/chi on the DEFAULT chi path
round1_2_raw.evidence: Python kraichnan_grad (line 177) computes S = chi*q/(kappa_T*kB**2) * k * exp(-sqrt(6q)*y), a simple-exponential form. The project's own MATLAB reference matlab/kraichnan_gradT.m computes S = chi*q/(3*kappa_T*kB^2) * k * (1 + sqrt(6q)*y) * exp(-sqrt(6q)*y) -- it carries a 1/3 normalization AND a (1 + sqrt(6q)*y) polynomial prefactor that Python omits. Both integrate to chi/(6*kappa_T) (verified numerically: ratios 1.0000), so total-variance chi recovery agrees when kB is exactly known, but the SHAPES differ materially: for kB=100 the Python form peaks at k=17.8 cpm while the standard/MATLAB form peaks at k=28.8 cpm (1.62x), and the shape ratio (py/standard) ranges from ~2.3x at y=0.05 to ~0.25x at y=2.0. Because the MLE/iterative fit infers kB (and hence epsilon_T = (2*pi*kB)^4*nu*kappa_T^2) from the resolved spectral SHAPE, the two implementations fit different kB/epsilon from identical observed spectra. kraichnan is the default spectrum_model at every entry point (rsi/config.py:54, perturb/config.py:119, chi_io.py:46/231, l4_chi.py:142/203), so this affects kB, epsilon_T, the unresolved-variance correction, FOM, and the output spec_batch for BOTH Method 1 and Method 2. git show abb1930 (PR #54 'Scientific-accuracy review') shows the (1+sqrt(6q)*y)/3 form was DELETED in favor of the simple exponential; the (1+...) form is the one used in Steinbuck et al. 2009, Peterson & Fer 2014, and the repo MATLAB code. No test cross-validates the Kraichnan shape against MATLAB (test_chi.py only checks the Python form against itself: integral=chi/6kappa and peak=kB/sqrt(6q) at lines 128-146), so the divergence is unguarded.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Resolve the Python-vs-MATLAB Kraichnan disagreement. The literature-standard 1D temperature-gradient Kraichnan spectrum (Steinbuck et al. 2009 eq. A6; Peterson & Fer 2014; matching matlab/kraichnan_gradT.m) is S(k)=chi*q/(3*kappa_T*kB^2)*k*(1+sqrt(6q)*y)*exp(-sqrt(6q)*y). Restore that form in kraichnan_grad (this is the higher-confidence correction), OR, if the simple-exponential form is intentionally chosen, fix the MATLAB reference to match and document the deviation. Either way add a test_matlab_chi cross-check that asserts kraichnan_grad(k,kB,chi) equals the MATLAB output within tolerance, and update test_chi.py::test_peak_location (line 128) which currently codifies the non-standard peak at kB/sqrt(6q). Verify the Peterson & Fer (2014) eq. 8 claim in docs/chi_mathematics.md:124 against the actual paper before keeping the current form.

round1_2_raw.title: Variance-correction returns NaN (no correction) for high-kB / tiny-K_max windows where the old grid produced a finite value -- a behavioral regression from PR #78
round1_2_raw.evidence: The new kB-scaled grid starts at K_fine[0] = K_upper/n = kB/_PTS_PER_KB = kB/2000. The resolved mask is (K_fine >= K_min) & (K_fine <= min(K_max, K_upper)); when K_max < kB/2000 the mask selects no grid points and the function returns NaN (verified: kB=2240,K_max=0.7 -> nan; kB=4000,K_max=1.0 -> nan). The pre-PR#78 code used K_fine starting at K_upper/n_fine*0.01 (~kB*2.5e-5) so the same case returned a finite correction. Both _iterative_fit (line 591-594) and _chi_from_epsilon (line 262-265) fall back to the UNcorrected chi when correction is NaN, so there is no crash, but the FP07-attenuation + unresolved-variance correction is silently dropped for these degenerate windows, under-estimating chi. Impact is low because these windows resolve essentially no signal (K_max ~ K[1] while kB is huge) and usually fail the min_points>=6 gate earlier, but the silent loss-of-correction is a genuine change from prior behavior.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: When K_max < K_fine[0] (i.e. min(K_max,K_upper) falls below the first grid point) but K_max > 0, either prepend a finer sub-grid over [K_min, K_max] for V_resolved, or clamp so at least the first bin near K_max is captured (e.g. ensure the grid's lowest point <= K_max, or compute V_resolved on a dedicated linspace(K_min, K_max, m)). Add a regression test for the high-kB/tiny-K_max case asserting a finite correction.

round1_2_raw.title: Stale code comment references non-existent function compute_chi_window
round1_2_raw.evidence: Line 30 comment '(matches compute_chi_window's fom_limit default)' and line 360 '...mirroring compute_chi_window's Method-1 filter...' reference a function compute_chi_window that no longer exists in the codebase (grep across src/ finds only these two comment references, no definition). The _EPS_FOM_LIMIT=1.15 value the comments justify is correct, but the cross-reference is dangling and will mislead future maintainers verifying the threshold's provenance.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Update both comments to point to the actual current source of the 1.15 epsilon-FOM limit (e.g. the Lueck 2022 FM QC limit cited in chi_io.py:562, or wherever the Method-1 probe filter now lives), or drop the stale function name.

round1_2_raw.title: write_patched_pfile lacks the header_size >= HEADER_BYTES guard, raising a cryptic struct.error on a corrupt/small header_size
round1_2_raw.evidence: write_patched_pfile reads header_size = words[_H["config_size"]] etc. but never validates header_size >= HEADER_BYTES, unlike extract_pfile_segment (p_file.py:138 raises 'invalid header_size'). At line 527-528 it does `header_region = bytearray(f.read(header_size))` then `struct.pack_into(f"{endian}H", header_region, _H["config_size"]*2, len(new_bytes))`. _H['config_size']*2 = 22, so any advertised header_size in [12,23] yields a buffer shorter than offset 22+2. Reproduced on a crafted .p with header_size=20: write_patched_pfile raises `struct.error: pack_into requires a buffer of at least 24 bytes for packing 2 bytes at offset 22 (actual buffer size is 20)` — an uncaught, cryptic crash. _cmd_patch_config only catches (FileNotFoundError, FileExistsError, ValueError, OSError) (cli.py:291), so struct.error escapes to a full traceback.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Add the same guard extract_pfile_segment uses, before any seek/pack: `if header_size < HEADER_BYTES: raise ValueError(f"{src.name}: invalid header_size={header_size}")`. Apply it in both write_patched_pfile and read_config_text (which shares the same unvalidated read).

round1_2_raw.title: read_config_text silently returns garbage/truncated config on a corrupt header_size or oversized config_size
round1_2_raw.evidence: read_config_text does `f.seek(header_size); return f.read(config_size).decode('latin-1')` with no validation of header_size (>= HEADER_BYTES) or that header_size+config_size fits the file. Reproduced: a .p advertising header_size=20 returns 40 bytes of binary header content decoded as 'config' (`'\x00\x06(\x00...'`) with no error. Because patch_files calls read_config_text → edit_config_text → write_patched_pfile, a corrupt source can silently flow garbage 'active' config text into the patched output. f.read(config_size) at EOF also returns fewer bytes than requested without error, so an oversized config_size silently yields a truncated config that is then re-emitted as the patched config.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Validate header_size >= HEADER_BYTES and header_size+config_size <= file_size in read_config_text, and verify len(read) == config_size, raising ValueError otherwise. Reuse the same checks as extract_pfile_segment / the _read() loader for consistency.

round1_2_raw.title: write_patched_pfile is not atomic: a mid-copy failure leaves a corrupt, valid-looking patched .p in the final destination
round1_2_raw.evidence: The function opens the FINAL destination with `open(dst, "xb")` and streams the source data in 1 MiB chunks with no try/except and no temp-file-then-rename. (Verified: inspect.getsource shows no 'except'/'unlink'/'os.remove'.) If the source read or destination write fails partway (disk full, I/O error, interrupt) after the header+config have been written, dst is left as a truncated file whose header still advertises the full config and (stale) record_size — i.e. a file that opens and parses but is missing data records. _cmd_patch_config catches OSError and prints an error (cli.py:291) but never removes the partial dst, so the user is left with a silently-corrupt 'patched' .p. The PR-#78-era hardening (0dc89c7) added the `xb` exclusive-create for TOCTOU but did not make the write atomic against mid-stream failure.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Write to a sibling temp file (e.g. dst.with_suffix(dst.suffix + '.tmp.<pid>') opened 'xb') and os.replace(tmp, dst) only after the full copy and flush/os.fsync succeed; on any exception, unlink the temp file. os.replace is atomic on the same filesystem and preserves the no-overwrite intent if you stat dst first.

round1_2_raw.title: patch_files aborts the whole batch (and leaves the first output written) when two source files share a basename
round1_2_raw.evidence: dst = out_dir / src.name uses only the basename. A glob spanning two directories (e.g. dirA/SAME.p and dirB/SAME.p — both are independently valid VMP files) maps both inputs to the same out_dir/SAME.p. Reproduced: the first file is written to outX/SAME.p, the second hits the `if dst.exists(): raise FileExistsError(...)` (line 655) / the `xb` create and the entire patch_files run aborts with 'outX/SAME.p already exists; remove it or choose another --out'. The first output is already on disk, the remaining inputs are silently unprocessed, and the error message points at a stale-output cause rather than the real basename collision. _cmd_patch_config converts this to a one-line CLI error with no indication that some files were patched and others skipped.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Detect basename collisions up front (e.g. before the loop, raise if len({s.name for s in srcs}) != len(srcs) with a message naming the colliding paths), or disambiguate outputs by including a parent-dir component / a uniquifying suffix. At minimum, make the per-file write a recoverable error that continues the batch and reports a summary, so one collision does not strand a half-finished run.

round1_2_raw.title: round(v, 10) collapses tiny float config values (epsilon_minimum/chi_minimum = 1e-13) to 0.0, corrupting the version hash and signature provenance
round1_2_raw.evidence: _normalize_value uses decimal-place rounding `return round(v, 10)`, which zeroes any value with |v| < ~5e-11. The perturb DEFAULTS set `epsilon.epsilon_minimum = 1e-13` and `chi.chi_minimum = 1e-13` (perturb/config.py:94,125). Verified: `_normalize_value(1e-13)` -> 0.0; `compute_hash('epsilon',{'epsilon_minimum':1e-13})` == `compute_hash('epsilon',{'epsilon_minimum':5e-13})` (both hash as 0.0), and `canonicalize('chi',{})` writes `chi_minimum: 0.0` into the signature JSON. Two consequences: (a) distinct dissipation floors anywhere in [-5e-11,5e-11] (the physically meaningful 1e-13 W/kg and K^2/s range) collapse to one output directory, so changing the floor silently REUSES stale output instead of recomputing; (b) the .params_sha256 signature records `0.0` while write_resolved_config writes the true `1e-13` to config.yaml, so the hash provenance and the human-readable config disagree. No test covers this; test_config.py:68 actually asserts the round(v,10) behavior for a 0.12-magnitude value.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Normalize floats by significant figures, not decimal places, so magnitude is preserved: replace `return round(v, 10)` with `return float(f"{v:.10e}")`. Verified this keeps 1e-13 and 5e-13 distinct, still collapses 256.0->256 (caught by the earlier `v == int(v)` branch), and round-trips 0.123456789012345 to 0.12345678901. Add a regression test asserting compute_hash differs for epsilon_minimum 1e-13 vs 5e-13 and that the signature JSON records the true 1e-13.

round1_2_raw.title: Sequential output-dir glob `{prefix}_[0-9][0-9]` only matches 2-digit suffixes; once >100 distinct configs accumulate, dir 100 is invisible to the scan, causing reuse-failure and a hard data-corruption collision
round1_2_raw.evidence: resolve_output_dir scans with `pattern = str(base / f"{prefix}_[0-9][0-9]")` (line 241) but creates dirs with `{next_seq:02d}` (line 256), which for next_seq>=100 emits a 3-digit name (e.g. eps_100). The glob `eps_[0-9][0-9]` then never matches eps_100. Verified end-to-end: after pre-populating eps_00..eps_99 with distinct signatures, the 101st distinct config creates eps_100; the 102nd distinct config (fft_length=6000) ALSO resolves to eps_100 (max_seq recomputes to 99 because eps_100 is unseen, next_seq=100 again, mkdir(exist_ok=True) reuses it). The dir then holds TWO conflicting .params_sha256 signatures (5000 and 6000) and two parameter sets' NetCDF outputs co-mingled. Separately, a re-run requesting the exact config stored in eps_100 fails to find it and creates a fresh eps_01, orphaning eps_100's data. Trigger requires >100 distinct configs in one output_root (parameter sweeps over a campaign).
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Make the scan glob width-adaptive and anchored: `pattern = str(base / f"{prefix}_[0-9][0-9]*")`. Verified it matches eps_00, eps_99, eps_100 while still excluding eps_binned_* and eps_xx; the existing `int(dp.name.split('_')[-1])` parse already handles 3+ digits. Optionally widen the format to a stable width or assert next_seq<=99.

round1_2_raw.title: hash_exclude_keys (intended for parameter keys like 'diagnostics') is incorrectly applied to top-level keys of dynamic-key sections, so an instrument literally named 'diagnostics' is silently dropped from the hash and canonical config
round1_2_raw.evidence: In _canonicalize_section the dynamic-key branch filters `if k not in self.hash_exclude_keys` (line 173) where k is an instrument *name* (serial-number folder key), but hash_exclude_keys={'diagnostics'} is meant to exclude the per-section diagnostics *toggle* in STATIC sections. Verified: `canonicalize('instruments', {'diagnostics': {'exclude_shear_probes': ['sh1']}})` == `canonicalize('instruments', {})` -> the instrument override vanishes from the hash. Likelihood is very low (instrument keys are SN-style names), but the semantic conflation is real.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Do not apply hash_exclude_keys in the dynamic-key branch (instrument names are not parameter keys). Drop the `if k not in self.hash_exclude_keys` filter from the dynamic branch of _canonicalize_section, or scope hash_exclude_keys to static sections only.

round1_2_raw.title: merge_config silently discards ALL overrides for dynamic-key sections (empty {} defaults), losing user data if ever called on 'instruments'
round1_2_raw.evidence: merge_config is unaware of self.dynamic_key_sections: it seeds `merged = dict(self.defaults[section])` (line 152, == {} for instruments) and only keeps keys where `k in merged` (lines 156,161). Verified `merge_config('instruments', {'SN465': {'exclude_shear_probes': ['sh1']}})` returns `{}` — the entire override is dropped. Not triggered today (perturb consumes instruments via config.get('instruments') and _canonical_instruments_for_hash, never merge_config), but it is a silent-data-loss trap for any future caller and inconsistent with the rest of ConfigManager which special-cases dynamic sections.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Either short-circuit dynamic sections in merge_config (e.g. `if section in self.dynamic_key_sections: return {**(file_values or {}), **(cli_overrides or {})}` with None-filtering) or raise a clear ValueError that merge_config is undefined for dynamic-key sections, so misuse fails loudly instead of returning {}.

round1_2_raw.title: Bottom-crash trim keeps the shallow ~half of the spike bin (sample-mean depth still leaves seafloor-impact-contaminated data in epsilon/chi)
round1_2_raw.evidence: PR #78 (M4) changed the flagged-bin report from the bin geometric center to `np.nanmean(depth[in_range & (idx == i)])` (the bin's sample mean). The caller (perturb/pipeline.py:432-438) trims with `below = np.where(P_seg >= bottom_depth)[0]; candidate = new_s + below[0] - 1`, i.e. it removes only samples at/below that mean depth. The bottom CRASH onset is at the SHALLOWEST edge of the spike bin, not its mean: the deepest bin spans [bins[i], bins[i+1]) and the loop flags it because its vibration std exceeds the threshold over the WHOLE bin. Reporting the sample mean (roughly mid-bin) keeps every spike-bin sample that lies above the mean — up to ~half a depth_window (~2 m by default) of genuinely impact-contaminated shear/temperature survives into the dissipation estimate, which biases epsilon/chi HIGH near the seafloor. The fix correctly solved the prior 'center below deepest sample' over-read, but swung past the conservative target. The minimum observed depth of the spike bin removes ALL crash-bin samples while staying within observed depths (so it is still robust to the fast/slow rate mismatch the comment cites).
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Return the shallowest observed depth of the flagged bin instead of its mean: `sel_depth = depth[in_range & (idx == i)]; return float(np.nanmin(sel_depth))`. This trims the entire spike bin (the crash region) while remaining within the bin's real samples, so the caller's `P_seg >= bottom_depth` removes all impact-contaminated data rather than half of it. (`nanmin` over the same non-empty finite selection is as safe as the current `nanmean`.) If keeping some shallow margin is intentional, document the rationale explicitly in the code comment.

round1_2_raw.title: compute_hash/canonicalize crash on mixed-type dict keys (numeric + string instrument serials, hotel.channels, qc.rules)
round1_2_raw.evidence: _normalize_nested (config_base.py:51) does `sorted(v.items())` and the dynamic-key branch of _canonicalize_section (config_base.py:173) does `sorted((params or {}).items())`. Python's sort compares keys directly, so any user-keyed dict containing BOTH a numeric and a string key raises `TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'str' and 'int'`. This is reachable from real YAML: an unquoted serial `465:` parses to int while `SN479:` is str. Confirmed: `pc.compute_hash('instruments', {465:{'exclude_shear_probes':['sh2']}, 'SN479':{...}})` raises TypeError; the full pipeline path also crashes — `pl._upstream_for('diss', {'instruments': {465:..., 'SN479':...}})` raises TypeError via _canonical_instruments_for_hash (pipeline.py:138, same `sorted(...items())`). The crash also fires for static sections with user-keyed nested dicts: `pc.compute_hash('hotel', {'channels': {1:{'name':'a'}, 'T1':{'name':'b'}}})` and `pc.compute_hash('qc', {'rules': {1:{'min':0}, 'r2':{...}}})` both raise. validate_config and load_config accept these configs without complaint (they check key names/structure, not key types), so the failure surfaces deep in resolve_output_dir as an opaque traceback that aborts the entire run. All-int or all-str key sets work; only the MIXED case crashes, making it insidious — a config that works with one instrument breaks the moment a second, differently-typed serial is added. No existing test covers mixed/numeric instrument keys (all use string 'SN465').
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Sort by the string form of the key everywhere a user-keyed dict is canonicalized: config_base.py:51 `{str(k): _normalize_nested(val) for k, val in sorted(v.items(), key=lambda kv: str(kv[0]))}`; config_base.py:173 `sorted((params or {}).items(), key=lambda kv: str(kv[0]))`; and mirror in pipeline.py:138 `sorted((instruments or {}).items(), key=lambda kv: str(kv[0]))`. Optionally also coerce numeric YAML serial keys to str at load time in _validate_instruments and warn on int/str collisions (`465` and `"465"`). Add a test with `{465: {...}, 'SN479': {...}}` to test_perturb_config.py covering compute_hash and resolve_output_dir.

round1_2_raw.title: Concurrent runs of two different configs against the same output_root can collide into one directory (no lock / no O_EXCL)
round1_2_raw.evidence: resolve_output_dir performs a non-atomic glob->check-signature->mkdir sequence with no file lock (grep for lock/fcntl/flock/O_EXCL in config_base.py, rsi/config.py, perturb/config.py returns nothing) and uses `mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)` (line 257). If two processes run DIFFERENT configs simultaneously against the same `base`, both compute distinct hashes, both see `max_seq=-1` (or the same max), both select the same `{prefix}_NN`, both mkdir it (exist_ok suppresses the error), and both write_signature into it — so two different parameter signatures and two configs' outputs share one directory. A later reuse scan then matches whichever signature it finds first, silently associating outputs with the wrong config. This requires two simultaneous invocations sharing one output root (e.g. a user running rsi-tpw twice, or two pipeline drivers), so it is niche, but the failure is silent data co-mingling rather than a crash.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Make directory claiming atomic: attempt `new_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=False)` in a loop, incrementing next_seq on FileExistsError until a fresh dir is created, then write the signature; or take an advisory lock (filelock / fcntl.flock on a `base/.resolve.lock`) around the scan+create critical section. At minimum, after creating with exist_ok=True, verify the dir contains no foreign `.params_sha256_*` and re-resolve if it does.

round1_2_raw.title: numpy scalar config values pass through normalization unconverted and crash json.dumps in compute_hash
round1_2_raw.evidence: _normalize_value special-cases Python `int`/`float` but `np.int64` is not an instance of Python `int` (confirmed `isinstance(np.int64(256), int)` is False), so it falls through to `return v` (line 41) unchanged; the subsequent `json.dumps` in canonicalize then raises `TypeError: Object of type int64 is not JSON serializable`. np.float64 happens to subclass float and is handled. Currently NOT reachable: every caller (rsi/cli.py:202, perturb/pipeline.py _setup_output_dirs/_upstream_for) feeds `merge_config` output derived from YAML/CLI native types, and a grep of the hashing callers shows no numpy/array/.values values entering. Flagged only as a defensive gap should config params ever be built from data arrays (e.g. a future geospatial auto-fill feeding gps.lat from a numpy array).
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: In _normalize_value, broaden the numeric checks to cover numpy scalars, e.g. `if isinstance(v, numbers.Integral): return int(v)` and `if isinstance(v, numbers.Real): ...` (import numbers), or explicitly call `.item()` on objects exposing it. This guarantees JSON-serializable output regardless of the scalar's concrete type.

round1_2_raw.title: fp07_model config value is never validated; a typo silently selects an inconsistent double-pole transfer + single-pole (lueck) tau combination
round1_2_raw.evidence:
Unlike spectrum_model (chi.py:82 raises ValueError on unknown) and the tau model (fp07.py:129 raises ValueError), the fp07_model string ('single_pole'/'double_pole') is never validated anywhere. It is read straight from YAML config (rsi/config.py:50,113) and threaded into THREE else-fallthrough branches that each silently default to the wrong physics on any typo:
  (1) default_tau_model (fp07.py:82): `return 'goto' if fp07_model=='double_pole' else 'lueck'`
  (2) fp07_transfer_batch (fp07.py:177-178): `if model=='single_pole': ...single... else: ...double-pole...`
  (3) l4_chi.py:88: `_h2 = fp07_transfer if l3_chi.fp07_model=='single_pole' else fp07_double_pole`
validate_config (config_base.py:117-133) checks only section/key NAMES, never values, so `fp07_model: single` (or 'singlepole', 'SinglePole') passes validation. I confirmed the concrete consequence: a typo of 'single' yields tau_model='lueck' (speed-dependent single-pole tau) PAIRED WITH the double-pole |H|^2 (verified: H2==fp07_double_pole, H2!=fp07_transfer). That is neither the intended single_pole config NOR the valid double_pole(goto, fixed 3ms) config -- it is a physically inconsistent mix applied silently to the FP07 attenuation correction (H2 enters both the spectra and _variance_correction), biasing chi/kB with no error or warning.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Validate fp07_model against {'single_pole','double_pole'} at the entry point (e.g. raise ValueError in fp07_transfer_batch's else branch like fp07_tau does, and/or add a value check in validate_config / process_l3_chi). Mirror the existing `raise ValueError(f"Unknown FP07 model: {model!r}")` pattern so a config typo fails loudly instead of silently mixing tau and transfer-function models.

round1_2_raw.title: _variance_correction leaks a NumPy RuntimeWarning on degenerate kB=inf (np.trapezoid subtract not inside errstate)
round1_2_raw.evidence: batchelor_grad/kraichnan_grad sanitize their own divide/invalid warnings, but in _variance_correction the grid `K_fine = np.arange(1,n+1)*(K_upper/n)` with K_upper=40*kB becomes all-inf when kB=inf, and the subsequent `np.trapezoid(spec_fine, K_fine)` performs an inf-inf subtract that emits 'RuntimeWarning: invalid value encountered in subtract' to the user (confirmed by catching it under simplefilter('error')). The kB>0 guard at chi.py:108 lets +inf through. In the real pipeline this is unreachable (callers guard kB<1 at chi.py:230/_iterative_fit:557 and the MLE kB grid is bounded to ~31623 cpm), so it only fires under direct misuse, but the leaked warning is noise an audited numerical kernel should not produce.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Tighten the guard to `if not (0 < kB < np.inf): return np.nan`, or wrap the K_fine construction + np.trapezoid calls in `with np.errstate(invalid='ignore'):` so a degenerate kB returns NaN cleanly without emitting a warning.

round1_2_raw.title: fp07_transfer_batch silently treats any unrecognized model string as double_pole (no validation), unlike fp07_tau
round1_2_raw.evidence: `if model == 'single_pole': return single...; else: return double-pole...`. Any value other than the exact string 'single_pole' (e.g. 'double-pole' with a hyphen, 'doublepole', or an empty string) is silently routed to the double-pole branch. fp07_tau (fp07.py:128-129) by contrast raises ValueError on an unknown model. This is the proximate mechanism behind the fp07_model finding above but is independently worth a guard: an internal caller passing a near-miss string gets a different |H|^2 with no diagnostic. Verified: fp07_transfer_batch(F, tau0, model='SinglePole') == fp07_double_pole output.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Make the branch explicit and validating: `if model=='single_pole': ...; elif model=='double_pole': ...; else: raise ValueError(f"Unknown FP07 model: {model!r}")`, matching fp07_tau's contract.

round1_2_raw.title: merge: no time-continuity/restarted check — 29 independent ARCTERX casts fuse into one chain (regressed/unfixed audit #1)
round1_2_raw.evidence: find_mergeable_files chains files purely on (config_hash, endian, record_size, header_size, config_size) + sequential file_number, with NO start-time continuity, gap tolerance, or restarted-flag gate. The 2026-06-19 audit flagged this exact issue at merge.py:99-114 as its top merge Major. Only the sibling header_size/config_size key fix (#55) was applied (test_different_header_size_not_merged exists); the continuity fix was never made. Verified live on the production data: `find_mergeable_files(sorted(Path('VMP').glob('*.p')))` returns ONE 29-file chain (0002..0030). The files are clearly independent casts, not size-limit rollovers: file 0002 starts 2025-01-14 15:30:17 and records 175.7 min, then file 0003 starts 2025-01-15 14:00:18 — a 22.50-hour gap; 0003->0004 is a 4.01-hour gap; even the tightest pair (0008 records 36.1 min, 0009 starts 40 min later) is non-contiguous. A genuine rollover would have the continuation start ~where the prior file's recording ended (gap ~0). restarted==0 for all files, so the header's restarted flag is NOT a usable continuity discriminator here; the inter-file start-time gap is. With files.merge=true (config files: merge), merge_p_files byte-concatenates all 29 casts into a single .p whose header time/geometry come from cast 0002, destroying per-cast structure and assigning one (wrong) time base to 24h+ of data. Default is merge:false so this is opt-in, hence major not blocker.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Before chaining group[i] onto chain[-1], require recording continuity: compute prev_end = prev.start_time + prev_n_scans/fs_fast and only chain when abs(next.start_time - prev_end) <= a tolerance (e.g. a few seconds / a few records). Compute start_time and duration in _read_merge_info from the header date words + (file_size-first_record)//record_size scans and clock_hz/clock_frac/n_cols (same arithmetic PFile._read uses). Reject the chain link otherwise (start a new chain). Optionally also require restarted==0 on the continuation as a secondary guard, but the time-gap test is the load-bearing one. Add a test mirroring the ARCTERX gap pattern (sequential file_number but multi-hour start gap -> no chain).

round1_2_raw.title: merge: no record-boundary validation of base or continuation files before splicing
round1_2_raw.evidence: merge_p_files copies chain[0] in full via copyfileobj then, for each continuation, seeks past header_size+config_size and copyfileobj's the remainder to EOF. There is no check that (file_size - first_record_size) % record_size == 0 for the base, nor that (file_size - skip) % record_size == 0 for any continuation. If trim is disabled (files.trim:false, which is independently configurable from files.merge) or a non-final file in the chain carries a fractional trailing record, those leftover bytes are appended verbatim and shift every subsequent file's records by a sub-record offset. The merged file's size is then not an integer number of records relative to chain[0]'s geometry; PFile._read only warns about a *trailing* partial record (p_file.py:352) and silently mis-slices the misaligned interior records with no error. _read_merge_info already reads record_size, so the alignment is cheap to assert.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: In merge_p_files (or _read_merge_info), validate each member: (file_size - header_size - config_size) % record_size == 0; raise/skip-with-log on violation, or copy only the integer-record prefix (skip + (n_complete*record_size)) of each continuation instead of to EOF. This makes the splice robust to an un-trimmed fractional file regardless of the files.trim setting.

round1_2_raw.title: config: files.merge documented with no warning about the cast-fusion / continuity hazard
round1_2_raw.evidence: The example config (config.py:323) lists `merge: false` with no comment, unlike the adjacent `force_trim` (322) which is annotated. Given that enabling merge on a typical multi-cast deployment directory (where casts share one config hash and carry sequential file_number) currently fuses all casts into one file (see the merge.py continuity finding), an operator has no in-config signal that merge is only safe for true size-limit rollovers.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Annotate line 323, e.g. `merge: false   # only for size-limit rollovers (contiguous in time); fuses casts otherwise`. Best paired with the continuity fix so the flag becomes safe to enable broadly.

round1_2_raw.title: _mle_fit_kB applies the unresolved-variance correction over [0, K_max] but integrates observed variance over [K_fit_low, K_max], biasing chi low for low-epsilon windows
round1_2_raw.evidence: In _mle_fit_kB the correction is computed as `_variance_correction(kB_best, K_max_fit, speed, tau0, _h2, grad_func)` with the default `K_min=0.0` (chi.py:440-447), so V_resolved integrates the model spectrum from the lowest grid wavenumber (~kB/2000, effectively 0) up to K_max. But `obs_var` is `trapezoid(max(spec_obs[fit_mask]-noise, 0), K[fit_mask])` (chi.py:449-451), whose lower bound is the first wavenumber above 2x noise (`_valid_wavenumber_mask`), i.e. K_fit_low > 0. The correction factor V_total/V_resolved therefore under-counts the missing band: V_resolved includes the model variance in [0, K_fit_low] that obs_var excludes, inflating V_resolved and so under-correcting chi. I quantified the affected band with batchelor_grad: for kB=200 cpm and K_fit_low=1-2 cpm the model variance below K_fit_low is <0.11% (negligible), but for low-epsilon windows (kB=30 cpm) where noise pushes K_fit_low up to 5-10 cpm, the model puts 21-54% of total variance below K_fit_low, so V_resolved is inflated by a comparable factor and chi is under-estimated by up to ~2x in exactly the low-signal windows where the correction matters most. The default chi path (`fit_method='iterative'`, l4_chi.py:204,226) is NOT affected because `_iterative_fit` correctly passes `K_min=k_l_new` matching its `chi_band` band (chi.py:588-589). This locus is reached only via the non-default `fit_method='mle'` path (l4_chi.py:259-270) and as the pre-fit grid-centre in `_chi_from_epsilon` (chi.py:261, where it is harmless because a log-LSQ fit follows). PR #78's grid change did not introduce this; the missing K_min is pre-existing.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Pass the same lower band edge to the correction that obs_var uses, e.g. `K_min = K[fit_mask][0]` (or `K_fit[0]`): `_variance_correction(kB_best, K_max_fit, speed, tau0, _h2, grad_func, K_min=float(K[fit_mask][0]))`. This makes V_resolved span the identical [K_fit_low, K_max] band as obs_var so the ratio purely corrects the truly-unresolved tails plus in-band |H|^2, matching the already-correct _iterative_fit treatment.

round1_2_raw.title: pair_nearest silently drops all pairings when the source-time median spacing is zero (degenerate/duplicate epsilon window times)
round1_2_raw.evidence: When `max_dt is None`, the tolerance defaults to `float(np.median(np.diff(np.sort(src_times))))` (mixing.py:512-513). If more than half of the consecutive source-time differences are zero (e.g. duplicated/clamped window centre times), the median diff is exactly 0.0, so `ok = dt <= max_dt` becomes `dt <= 0` and only destinations landing *exactly* on a source time are paired; every other destination silently gets NaN. I reproduced this: `pair_nearest([5,5,5],[1,2,3],[5.0])` returns `[1.]` only because the destination exactly equals the (duplicated) source time; a destination at 5.1 would yield NaN even though a source sits 0.1 s away. In production `l4.time` are strictly increasing window centres so this is latent, not currently triggered, but it is a silent-total-data-loss failure mode rather than a safe fallback.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Guard the auto tolerance against a non-positive median, e.g. `med = float(np.median(np.diff(np.sort(src_times))))`; `max_dt = med if (len(src_times) > 1 and med > 0) else 30.0` (or some sensible positive floor), so a degenerate cadence falls back to a usable tolerance instead of 0.

round1_2_raw.title: _variance_correction n_fine/_N_FINE floor is dead code (always dominated by _K_SPAN_KB*_PTS_PER_KB)
round1_2_raw.evidence: Line 113 computes `n = max(int(n_fine), _K_SPAN_KB * _PTS_PER_KB)`. With the module constants `_N_FINE=2000`, `_K_SPAN_KB=40`, `_PTS_PER_KB=2000`, the second term is 80000, which always dominates the 2000 floor, so the `n_fine` parameter (default `_N_FINE`) and the `int(n_fine)` floor never affect the grid size. The docstring at chi.py:111 states 'n_fine is a floor', which is technically true but never binding and is therefore misleading. This is a cosmetic/clarity issue from the PR #78 grid rework, not a correctness defect (the grid is fine).
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Either drop the now-inert `n_fine` parameter and the `max(int(n_fine), ...)` (use `n = _K_SPAN_KB * _PTS_PER_KB` directly), or, if keeping the knob for callers, raise `_N_FINE` to a value that can actually bind and update the docstring; otherwise remove the dead floor to avoid implying tunability that does not exist.

round1_2_raw.title: Method-1 epsilon averaging filters the wrong FOM statistic (variance-ratio fom vs Lueck FM); the 1.15 limit is defined for FM, silently dropping good shear probes
round1_2_raw.evidence: In `_epsilon_ds_to_l4data`, the `n_sh > 1` fallback path reads `fom_arr = _probe_var("fom")` (line 353) and rejects probes with `fom_arr > _EPS_FOM_LIMIT` where `_EPS_FOM_LIMIT = 1.15` (lines 31, 365-366). But the epsilon NetCDF written by `rsi-tpw eps` carries TWO distinct statistics: the variance-ratio `fom` (dissipation.py:455-465: "observed/Nasmyth variance ratio; values near 1.0 indicate a good fit", centered on 1.0 and routinely 0.7-1.4 for perfectly good fits) and the MAD-based Lueck (2022) `FM` (dissipation.py:468-481: "Good fits approach 0; ATOMIX recommends rejecting FM > ~1.15"). The 1.15 limit belongs to FM, NOT to the variance-ratio fom. Two independent code paths confirm this: (1) the canonical QC path `scor160/l4.py:213-216` sets `fom_for_flags = FM` and explicitly comments "Flag on the MAD-based FM statistic ... the ATOMIX convention the fom_limit default of 1.15 was written for"; (2) the interactive viewer `rsi/viewer_base.py:183-186` passes `result.get("FMs")` as the `fom` argument to `compute_chi_window` with the comment "The 1.15 QC limit is defined for the MAD-based Lueck FM statistic, not the variance-ratio fom." `_epsilon_ds_to_l4data` instead applies the 1.15 limit to the variance-ratio `fom`, so any genuinely good shear probe whose variance-ratio fom happens to exceed 1.15 (a common, fully acceptable value) is marked `bad` and dropped from the Method-1 mean. When one of two probes is dropped, `epsi_final` is biased toward the surviving probe; this epsilon sets kB via `batchelor_kB` and the unresolved-variance correction in `_chi_from_epsilon`, so chi is silently biased. This is production-reachable: the rsi pipeline epsilon output has no `epsilonMean`, SN479 has 2 shear probes (n_sh=2>1), so `_compute_chi_one` -> `_epsilon_ds_to_l4data` takes exactly this branch. The dataset also contains the correct `FM` variable, which is read by neither `_probe_var` call.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Filter against the Lueck FM statistic, not the variance-ratio fom. Read `FM` (with fallback to `fom` only when `FM` is absent, mirroring l4.py's `fom_for_flags = FM if num_ffts > 0 else fom`): e.g. `fm_arr = _probe_var("FM"); filt_arr = fm_arr if fm_arr is not None else fom_arr` and use `filt_arr > _EPS_FOM_LIMIT` for the `bad` mask. Update the comment at lines 29-31/359-366 to name FM, and fix `tests/test_chi.py::test_high_fom_probe_excluded_from_method1_mean` (line 885-908) and `test_all_probes_bad_fom_fall_back_to_all_mean` (910-925), which currently encode the same mislabeling by putting the reject value in a `fom` variable; they should populate `FM`.

round1_2_raw.title: visc() Sharqawy branch lacks the negative-viscosity floor that visc35 has, crashing nasmyth on a spurious-cold window mean
round1_2_raw.evidence: visc35 was hardened with a positive floor specifically because nasmyth() raises ValueError on nu<=0 (nasmyth.py:36-37 `if nu <= 0: raise ValueError`), and a single spurious-hot sample drove the cubic negative (ocean.py:32-37 comment, test_high_temperature_stays_positive #39). The PARALLEL Sharqawy branch used for all salinity-aware production processing (dissipation.py:218 `visc(l3.temp, sal_window, l3.pres)` when salinity is not None; l3_chi.py:190) has NO such floor. The Sharqawy mu_w denominator 0.157*(T+64.993)^2 - 91.296 crosses zero near T=-41.9 C and mu (and hence nu=mu/rho) goes NEGATIVE below it. Verified: `visc(-42.0, 34, 100) = -1.235e-4` and `visc(-50.0, 34, 100) = -1.84e-5` (both negative). A window whose mean temperature is below ~-42 C (an all-fill-value window, e.g. -99 sentinels, or a frozen/dropped-out thermistor, or a corrupt L3 temp record) feeds a negative nu straight into the per-window epsilon loop (dissipation.py:225 `nu = nu_out[j]`), where nasmyth() then raises ValueError and aborts the whole file. The visc35 floor closed exactly this crash on the hot side; the cold side of the same hazard is left open on the branch that is actually the documented default path (the docstring at ocean.py:50-52 states in-situ P>0 keeps a cast on the Sharqawy branch).
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Mirror the visc35 hardening on the Sharqawy branch: floor the returned nu (e.g. `return np.maximum(mu / rho, 1.0e-7)`), and/or clamp/guard the mu_w denominator against the -42 C zero-crossing. A small positive floor well below realistic seawater viscosity leaves the 0-40 C ocean range untouched while preventing the negative-nu nasmyth crash, exactly as the floor on visc35 does. Add a regression test analogous to test_high_temperature_stays_positive but for a spurious-cold sample through the salinity branch.

round1_2_raw.title: buoyancy_freq default lat=0 biases N^2 via gravity (TEOS-10 g(lat)) for non-equatorial data
round1_2_raw.evidence: buoyancy_freq defaults `lat=0`. gsw.Nsquared scales N^2 by the latitude-dependent gravitational acceleration. Verified N2[0] = 2.4623e-4 at lat=0 vs 2.4854e-4 at lat=70 (a ~0.9% bias at high latitude; ~0.07% at the ARCTERX Saipan latitude ~15N). Unlike the production stratification path in processing/mixing.py (which threads a real `lat` through `_stable_window` -> gsw.Nsquared), this convenience function silently uses the equatorial gravity if the caller omits lat, so any user calling buoyancy_freq directly gets a systematically biased N^2 (and therefore biased Gamma/K_rho if they derive them) with no warning. Note this function currently has NO callers in src/ (only tests), so impact is latent.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Either drop the `lat=0` default and make lat a required argument (forcing the caller to supply the cast latitude, consistent with mixing.profile_stratification), or emit a one-time warning when lat is left at the default. Document that N^2 is gravity-scaled and lat materially affects it away from the equator.

round1_2_raw.title: buoyancy_freq produces inf N^2 on duplicate/non-monotonic input pressure (unguarded dp=0 division)
round1_2_raw.evidence: buoyancy_freq passes P straight to gsw.Nsquared with no monotonicity/spacing guard. gsw.Nsquared differentiates over dp between adjacent samples; a duplicate pressure (dp=0) yields a division by zero. Verified: with `p=[0,50,50,100]`, `gsw.Nsquared` returns `N2 = [2.464e-4, inf, 1.555e-4]` (a silent inf, not an error). Duplicate or repeated pressures are common in binned/decimated profiles and at turn-around points. The production path (processing/mixing.py `_stable_window`, line 314) guards this with `if p_pair[1]-p_pair[0] < min_dp/2: return nan`, but ocean.buoyancy_freq has no equivalent guard, so any direct caller silently propagates inf into N^2 (and thus Gamma/K_rho). Currently latent (no src callers), but it is a public, exported, tested API.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Before calling gsw.Nsquared, validate that P is strictly monotonic (or at least that no adjacent dp is <= a small floor); raise a clear ValueError or replace the offending N^2 entries with NaN, matching the min_dp guard that mixing.py already applies. At minimum, document the strict-monotonic-pressure precondition in the docstring.

round1_2_raw.title: ct_align discards an entire profile's lag contribution on a single non-finite C/T sample (over-discard)
round1_2_raw.evidence: The new guard `if not (np.all(np.isfinite(seg_T)) and np.all(np.isfinite(seg_C))): continue` drops the WHOLE profile segment if even one sample is non-finite. convert_jac_c (channels.py:238) emits NaN only on isolated corrupt samples (v_part==0), so a single bad sample in a multi-thousand-sample cast removes that entire profile from the weighted-median lag estimate. If several profiles each carry one sporadic NaN, the consensus can collapse to too-few/zero usable profiles -> `total<=0` -> `C.copy(), 0.0` (no alignment applied at all). The skip is correct vs. the pre-fix poisoning, but it is coarser than necessary.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Instead of skipping the whole segment, drop/interpolate only the non-finite samples before diff/lfilter (e.g. mask the bad indices in seg_T/seg_C, or linearly interpolate them) so a sporadic corrupt sample doesn't forfeit the entire profile's alignment information. Keep the all-NaN/short-segment skip as the fallback when too few finite samples remain.

round1_2_raw.title: _load_from_nc leaks the open NetCDF Dataset handle on every error/exception path
round1_2_raw.evidence: `ds = nc.Dataset(str(nc_path), "r")` (line 349) is only closed at line 458 on the success path. Any exception between open and close -- the `raise ValueError` at lines 385/416/418, an AttributeError from `_get_nc_attr`, or a KeyError on a missing TIME/PRES variable -- returns/propagates without `ds.close()`, leaking the file handle (and on some platforms holding a lock on the file). PR #78's `_nc_filled` additions did not change this, but they sit inside the same unprotected span. With batch/parallel extraction (`_extract_one`) this can accumulate descriptors.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Wrap the body in `try: ... finally: ds.close()` (or use `with nc.Dataset(str(nc_path), "r") as ds:`), so the handle is released on every exit path.

round1_2_raw.title: Dead `n_fine`/`_N_FINE` parameter in _variance_correction after the kB-scaled grid rewrite
round1_2_raw.evidence: After PR #78, `n = max(int(n_fine), _K_SPAN_KB * _PTS_PER_KB)` with `_K_SPAN_KB*_PTS_PER_KB = 40*2000 = 80000`, while the only value ever passed for `n_fine` is the default `_N_FINE = 2000`. The floor is therefore never active and `n_fine` no longer controls grid resolution for any caller (chi.py:261, 440, 588 all use the default). A future caller passing a large `n_fine` expecting finer resolution would silently get 80000 points regardless. The docstring line 111 ('``n_fine`` is a floor') documents this, but the parameter is effectively vestigial.
round1_2_raw.suggested_fix: Remove the `n_fine`/`_N_FINE` parameter (or rename to a clearly-internal floor and drop it from the public signature), and set `n = _K_SPAN_KB * _PTS_PER_KB` directly, to avoid the misleading appearance of a tunable resolution knob.
